Occupational Safety at Events

PPE, Hazards, Regulatory Standards and Emergency Protocols

"An accident at an event is not fate.

It is the consequence of uncontrolled risk."

📋  Occupational Safety at Events — What Is at Stake

The events sector is one of those that records the most workplace accidents in Brazil. Work at height, temporary electrical installations, handling of heavy loads, exposure to intense noise, use of pyrotechnic substances — all of this happens simultaneously, under schedule pressure, with teams hired for that single event.


The combination of pressure, temporary environments, diverse teams and short deadlines creates a high-risk environment that demands permanent professional awareness. Occupational safety at events is not the exclusive responsibility of the Safety Technician — it is the responsibility of every professional who sets foot on site.


In this lesson you will learn: what the main hazards are in each area of the event, which Regulatory Standards are mandatory, how PPE works and when each item should be used, the emergency protocols and how to report and prevent accidents. By the end, you will operate with risk awareness — and that saves lives.


⚠️  The numbers nobody wants to see — but needs to know

Brazil records more than 612,000 workplace accidents per year (AEAT/MTE). In the entertainment and events sector, the most lethal accidents are:

• Collapse of temporary structures (stages, tents, trusses)

• Falls from height during setup and rigging

• Electric shock in temporary installations

• Fire from irregular use of pyrotechnics or electrical failure

All preventable with planning, training and the correct PPE.

⚖️  THE REGULATORY STANDARDS — WHAT THE LAW REQUIRES


The Regulatory Standards (NRs) are legal instruments of the Ministry of Labor that establish safety obligations for employers and workers. In the context of events, several NRs apply simultaneously — and ignorance of the law does not exempt anyone from civil or criminal liability.


MANDATORY NRS AT EVENTS — COMPLETE REFERENCE


NR

STANDARD

MAIN OBLIGATION

ROLES AT EVENTS

NR-01

General Provisions and Risk Management (PGR)

Every employer must draw up and implement the Risk Management Program before activities begin.

All departments — responsibility of the Executive Producer

NR-04

SESMT — Specialized Service in Safety Engineering

Companies with more than 50 workers must maintain a SESMT with a safety technician and/or engineer.

Large production companies, structure companies

NR-05

CIPA — Internal Accident Prevention Committee

Companies with 20+ employees must form a CIPA with representatives of employers and employees.

Production companies and service-supply companies

NR-06

PPE — Personal Protective Equipment

The employer must provide, free of charge, PPE suited to the risk. The worker must use and maintain it.

All departments — mutual obligation

NR-07

PCMSO — Occupational Health Medical Control Program

Pre-employment, periodic, return-to-work and termination medical exams are mandatory for all workers.

All hired staff — responsibility of the employer

NR-09

Physical, Chemical and Biological Agents (PEO)

Identify and control exposure to noise, heat, vibration, chemical products and biological agents.

Sound, SFX, Kitchen, Cleaning, Electrical

NR-10

Safety in Electrical Installations

Work on electrical installations only by a qualified professional. NR-10 training is mandatory.

Electricians, lighting, video and sound technicians

Handling equipment (forklifts, hoists) only by certified operators.

Cleaning, F&B, reception, everyone

Work at Height

🦺  PPE — PERSONAL PROTECTIVE EQUIPMENT


PPE is the worker's last line of defense. Before it must come collective protection measures (machine guards, signage, barriers) and administrative measures (training, procedures). But when residual risk exists — and at events it always does — PPE is mandatory, free of charge and non-negotiable.


Every PPE item sold in Brazil must have the Certificate of Approval (CA) issued by the Ministry of Labor. PPE without a CA is not PPE — it is decorative equipment with no proven effectiveness. Always demand the CA number when receiving any PPE from the contractor.

🏗️  NR-35 — WORK AT HEIGHT: THE RISK THAT KILLS MOST AT EVENTS


NR-35 is the standard that most directly protects lives at events. Falls from height are the leading cause of death in event setup in Brazil and worldwide. Any activity carried out above 2 meters — installing a fixture, securing a speaker, assembling a truss — is work at height and requires full compliance with NR-35.


WHAT NR-35 REQUIRES — COMPLETE OBLIGATIONS


Mandatory training

Every worker who will work at height must hold an NR-35 training certificate with a minimum of 8 theoretical hours and 4 practical hours. Valid for 2 years.

Risk Analysis (RA)

Before any work at height, a documented Risk Analysis identifying the hazards and the necessary controls is mandatory.

Work Permit (WP)

For high-risk tasks, the Work Permit is a document that formally authorizes the activity after all safety requirements have been verified.

Mandatory fall-protection PPE

Full-body harness + double lanyard with shock absorber + helmet with chin strap + safety footwear. The entire set must have a valid CA.

Certified anchor point

The anchor point must withstand at least 15 kN (1,500 kg). Do not anchor to the handrail itself or to non-certified parts of the structure.

Inspection of PPE

Before each use, the worker must inspect the harness and lanyard: stitching, buckles, rings and shock absorber. PPE with visible damage must be replaced immediately.

Ban on the waist belt

The waist belt (simple positioning belt) is PROHIBITED for work at height — only the full-body harness. The waist belt does not restrain the worker in a free fall.

Working in pairs

It is always recommended to have a second worker on the ground as a safety observer and immediate support in case of a fall or emergency.


🔴  What most people ignore — and what kills

The simple waist belt (the one with a buckle and a single large ring) is PROHIBITED by law for work at height. It does not distribute the fall force across the body — in a free fall it tears through the abdominal region and can be fatal. Only the 4-ring full-body harness, with a double lanyard and shock absorber, is legal and safe for any work above 2 meters at events.


❌  INCORRECT PRACTICE

✅  CORRECT PRACTICE

Using a simple waist belt to climb the grid

4-ring full-body harness — the only one legal for height

Climbing the grid without a prior inspection of the PPE

Inspect the harness, lanyard and buckles before each use

Working at height without a documented Risk Analysis

RA completed and signed before starting any work at height

Anchoring the lanyard to a non-certified truss

Anchor point certified for 15 kN — check with the engineer

Working at height alone without an observer on the ground

Pair: worker at height + safety observer on the ground

Using height PPE without the Certificate of Approval (CA)

Demand the CA from the supplier — PPE without a CA has no proven effectiveness


⚡  NR-10 — SAFETY IN ELECTRICAL INSTALLATIONS


Events depend on large amounts of electrical power delivered by temporary installations — generators, power cables, distribution panels, extensions. Poorly executed temporary electrical installations are the leading cause of fires at events and one of the leading causes of fatal electric shock.


NR-10 OBLIGATIONS AT EVENTS


Who may work with electricity

Only a professional with NR-10 training (basic: 40h) or NR-10 with SEP (Power Electrical System: 40h+16h). An electrician without documented training is illegal.

LOTO — Lockout and Tagout

Before any maintenance or connection in an electrical installation, apply the lockout procedure (switch off, lock, tag) to ensure no one reactivates the power accidentally.

Safety distance

An unqualified professional must keep a minimum distance from energized installations. NR-10 defines the control zones by voltage level.

Mandatory grounding

Every electrical device at an event must have correct grounding. Equipment without grounding on a wet floor = guaranteed electric shock.

Extensions and power cables

Extensions must be sized for the load they will carry. An undersized cable overheats, melts the insulation and causes a fire.

Temporary distribution panel

The panel must have individual breakers per circuit, a residual-current device (RCD) and identification of each output. An improvised panel with electrical tape is a fire risk.

Mandatory electrical PPE

Insulating gloves (with CA), insulating boots (with CA), safety glasses, class B helmet and flame-resistant clothing (in high-voltage systems). All with a valid CA.

🚨  NR-23 AND THE BRIGADE — FIRE PREVENTION AND EMERGENCY PLAN


An event's emergency plan is not a document that sits in a drawer — it is a living protocol that the entire team must know by heart. In emergencies, the first 3 minutes determine whether the incident is managed or becomes a tragedy. There is no time to read a manual.


FIRE-PREVENTION OBLIGATIONS AT EVENTS — NR-23


Fire extinguishers

One extinguisher per 250 m² of usable area as a general rule. Type: CO₂ for electrical and ABC for common materials. Inspected and sealed within their validity period.

Escape routes

Marked with photoluminescent strips and emergency lighting. The whole team must know the exit routes before the event opens.

Fire brigade

Minimum composition defined by ABNT NBR 15,595 according to the number of people and type of event. Every brigade member with a valid certificate (annual refresher).

Fire Department permit

Mandatory for events above a certain audience size — it varies by state. Without the permit = illegal event.

Meeting point

An area outside the event designated as the meeting point during evacuation. The entire team must know where it is.

Emergency communication

A radio channel exclusively for emergencies. The producer, the safety coordinator and the brigade member must always be on this channel.

Pre-event drill

At large events, running an evacuation drill with the team before opening to the public is good practice and a requirement of some Fire Departments.


EMERGENCY PROTOCOL — 5 STEPS THE WHOLE TEAM MUST KNOW


STEP 1 — IDENTIFY

Confirm the type of emergency: fire, structural collapse, personal accident, crowd conflict. Do not act before identifying it.

STEP 2 — ALERT

Activate the emergency radio channel and inform the safety coordinator and the producer. State: what happened, where, how many people are involved.

STEP 3 — ISOLATE

Isolate the risk area immediately. No one enters the danger zone except those with the role and PPE to do so.

STEP 4 — EVACUATE

If necessary, begin an orderly evacuation via the escape routes. Brigade members direct the flow. No one runs — panic causes stampedes.

STEP 5 — EXTERNAL SUPPORT

If necessary, call the ambulance service (192), Fire Department (193) and Police (190). Assign a team member to receive and guide the external support to the site.

📋  HOW TO REPORT AN ACCIDENT — CAT AND INVESTIGATION


When an accident happens, the immediate response is to save lives. But right afterward there is a legal obligation many producers are unaware of: issuing the CAT — Workplace Accident Report — and investigating the accident so that it does not happen again.


THE CAT — WORKPLACE ACCIDENT REPORT


What it is

Official document that reports the occurrence of a workplace accident or occupational disease to the INSS. Mandatory by law (Art. 22 of Law 8.213/91).

When to issue it

On the first business day after the accident. In the event of death, immediate reporting — before the burial.

Who issues it

The employer of the injured worker. If the company refuses, the worker themselves, their dependent or the union may issue it.

How to issue it

Through the Federal Government portal (cat.inss.gov.br) or in person at an INSS branch. Free of charge.

Consequence of not issuing it

Administrative fine for the employer + loss of the worker's social-security rights (accident sickness benefit, 12-month job stability after medical discharge).

Post-accident job stability

A worker who has suffered a workplace accident has job stability for 12 months after medical discharge. Dismissal during this period is illegal.


ACCIDENT INVESTIGATION — THE OBLIGATION THAT PREVENTS THE NEXT ONE


Every accident — even without a serious victim — must be investigated to identify its causes and implement corrective measures. An investigated accident that leads to a change in procedure is the only one worth anything. An ignored accident is the announcement of the next one.


Who conducts it

The Occupational Safety Technician or Engineer, with the participation of the person responsible for the area where the accident occurred.

Recommended method

Cause-and-Effect Diagram (Ishikawa) or Fault Tree — identifies the immediate, underlying and root causes of the accident.

What it documents

What happened, where, when, who was involved, what the causes were, which PPE was being used (or not), and which corrective actions were implemented.

Deadline for action

Corrective actions must be implemented before the same risk activity is carried out again.

Record keeping

The investigation report must be kept on file for at least 5 years — it may be requested during a Ministry of Labor inspection.


✅  Rights of the injured worker — know yours

1. Immediate medical care paid for by the employer or access to the public health system as a workplace accident.

2. Issuance of the CAT — the document that guarantees your social-security rights.

3. Accident sickness benefit (B-91) from the INSS while on leave — with no waiting period.

4. 12-month job stability after receiving medical discharge.

5. The possibility of a lawsuit for compensation if the accident occurred due to the employer's negligence.

6. In the case of permanent incapacity: disability retirement or a death pension for dependents.

⚖️  RIGHTS AND DUTIES — WORKER AND EMPLOYER


Occupational safety is a relationship of shared responsibility. The employer has legal protection obligations. The worker has compliance obligations. And both have rights guaranteed by law. Knowing this balance is professional power.


OBLIGATIONS OF THE EMPLOYER / CONTRACTOR AT EVENTS


  • Provide, free of charge, the PPE suited to the risk of each role (NR-06).

  • Draw up the Risk Management Program (PGR) before activities begin (NR-01).

  • Ensure that workers at height have valid NR-35 training before climbing (NR-35).

  • Hire only electricians with documented NR-10 training for electrical installations (NR-10).

  • Carry out pre-employment exams before work begins and periodic exams during employment (NR-07).

  • Issue the CAT in the event of an accident — within the legal deadline and without pressuring the worker.

  • Ensure minimum sanitary conditions: restrooms, drinking water, a place to eat and rest (NR-24).

  • Investigate accidents and implement corrective measures before repeating the risk activity.


OBLIGATIONS OF THE WORKER AT EVENTS


  • Use the provided PPE correctly throughout the entire risk activity — it is not optional.

  • Immediately report to the supervisor any risk condition identified in your area.

  • Do not perform activities for which you have no training or authorization — even under pressure.

  • Attend the safety training offered by the employer — attendance is mandatory.

  • Maintain the PPE received and report damage or defects immediately for replacement.

  • Follow the established safety procedures — even when they seem unnecessary.


RIGHT OF REFUSAL — WHEN YOU CAN SAY NO


The worker has the legal right to refuse to perform an activity that poses a serious and imminent risk to their health or physical integrity — without suffering punishment or pay deduction. This right is set out in NR-01 and the CLT (Art. 483). Learn the situations in which you can and should exercise it:


Work at height without complete PPE

Refuse to climb. Report it to the supervisor and the Safety Technician. Document the refusal in writing if possible.

Electrical installation without an NR-10-qualified professional

Refuse any request for electrical work without qualification. Immediate risk of death.

Pyrotechnics operation without a license and a brigade on standby

Refuse the operation. Beyond the risk, it is a crime. You can be held liable alongside the producer.

PPE supplied without a Certificate of Approval (CA)

Demand the CA. PPE without a CA is ineffective. You have the right to certified PPE.

A load above the legal limit for manual lifting

Refuse or request mechanical assistance. NR-17 sets the limits — exceeding them risks permanent injury.


✅  SAFETY CHECKLIST — PRE-EVENT, EVENT DAY AND POST-EVENT


A safety checklist is not bureaucracy — it is the team's collective memory. At events with dozens of professionals operating simultaneously under pressure, the checklist ensures nothing critical is forgotten. Use this template as a basis for your event.


PRE-EVENT CHECKLIST — BEFORE SETUP BEGINS


PGR drawn up and distributed

The Risk Management Program was drawn up by the Safety Technician and distributed to all area coordinators.

NR-35 certificates verified

All workers who will work at height have a valid NR-35 certificate (less than 2 years old).

PPE verified and distributed

All PPE was verified (valid CA, no damage) and distributed to the corresponding roles before setup began.

Structure ART signed

The responsible engineer signed the ART before any use of the stage, tent or temporary structure.

Fire Department permit

Permit obtained and posted on site as required by the state.

Fire brigade confirmed

Minimum composition of brigade members confirmed, positioned and briefed.

Fire extinguishers inspected

All extinguishers checked: seal intact, pressure gauge in the green zone, within their validity period.

Escape routes marked

All emergency signage installed and emergency lighting tested.

Meeting point defined and communicated

The whole team knows where the meeting point is in case of evacuation.

Emergency radio configured

An exclusive emergency channel configured on all coordination radios.


EVENT-DAY CHECKLIST — DURING OPERATION


Safety rounds every 2h

The Safety Technician or safety coordinator makes periodic rounds of all areas, checking PPE use and risk conditions.

Brigade members at their posts

Check at every shift change that all brigade posts are staffed.

No unauthorized access to the grid/backstage

Credential control active at all access points to technical areas.

Extinguishers accessible

Extinguishers have not been covered, blocked or removed during setup.

SFX risk area isolated

Exclusion zone around the SFX equipment marked and respected before each operation.

Incident log up to date

Any incident (even a minor one) recorded in the logbook or incident app in real time.


POST-EVENT CHECKLIST — AFTER CLOSING


CAT issued (if there was an accident)

If any workplace accident occurred during the event, the CAT must be issued on the first business day.

PPE collected and inspected

All PPE returned, inspected and stored or discarded according to its condition.

Incident report closed

A report of all safety incidents during the event documented and filed.

Safety debriefing held

A closing meeting with the Safety Technician and coordinators to identify improvement points for the next event.

Accident investigation started

If there was an accident, the investigation started within 48h using the cause-analysis method.

🎯  Conclusion — Safety is not a cost. It is a culture.

Occupational safety at events is not a checklist to be completed to satisfy an inspection. It is a culture that begins when the producer draws up the PGR and ends when the last worker leaves the load-out safe and sound. Every NR, every PPE item, every protocol exists because someone, at some event, was injured or died for lack of them.


The professional trained by SMU Produções knows the risks of their role, knows which PPE to use, understands the legal hierarchy of safety and exercises their right of refusal when necessary. This is not excessive caution — it is the minimum standard of the professional market.


A safe event is not an event without risk. It is an event where the risk was identified, controlled and monitored by a team that knows what it is doing. You are now part of that team.


🏅  The 5 commitments of the SMU professional to safety

1. Never start work at height without a full-body harness, lanyard and a documented risk analysis.

2. Never handle electrical installations without NR-10 qualification — not even for 'just a second'.

3. Report any risk condition immediately to the supervisor — silence is complicity.

4. Demand the CA of the supplied PPE — PPE without a certificate does not protect.

5. Exercise the right of refusal when the risk is serious and imminent — it is legal, it is professional, it saves lives.

You arrive early to leave in one piece."

📋  Electrical Work as Infrastructure — Understanding the System

Every professional event is a temporary city in electrical terms. In a few hours, a team installs an electrical infrastructure capable of supplying from 50 kW to more than 2,000 kW — enough to power hundreds of homes. This infrastructure is set up, operated and dismantled under time pressure, with temporary equipment and teams that change with each event.


The difference between a correctly executed event electrical installation and a badly executed one is not visual — it is invisible until the moment the short circuit causes a fire, the incorrect grounding electrocutes the drummer on stage, or the undersized cable melts and shuts down the PA in the middle of the show. In this lesson you will learn it all: from the generator to the last terminal.


⚠️  NR-10 — Mandatory qualification before anything else

Every professional who works with electrical installations at events needs NR-10 training (minimum 40 hours). Those who operate near power electrical systems (generators, medium-voltage panels) need NR-10 with SEP (40h + 16 additional hours). Working with electricity without documented qualification is a crime. The contractor who allows it is as responsible as the worker.

🔋  GENERATORS — THE EVENT'S POWER SOURCE


The generator is the electrical heart of any event held at a venue without adequate utility infrastructure — and in many cases, even where there is a public grid, the generator is used as the primary or backup source. Choosing the wrong generator can mean power outages, equipment damage and the collapse of the operation.


GENERATOR TYPES AND THEIR APPLICATION AT EVENTS


GENERATOR TYPE

TECHNICAL SPECIFICATION

APPLICATION AT EVENTS

Open Generator (Skid)

Diesel engine coupled to an alternator, without a soundproof enclosure. Power from 20 to 2,000 kVA. Output voltage: 220V / 380V three-phase. Speed: 1,800 rpm (60 Hz). Electronic speed regulation (AVR).

Backstage far from the audience, an external generation area. Cheap, but very noisy (85-105 dB at 7 m). Requires acoustic barriers if near the event.

Silent Generator (Soundproofed)

Same engine and alternator as the open type, but enclosed in a steel cabin with sound-absorbing panels. Noise level: 65-75 dB at 7 m. Power: 20 to 1,500 kVA. Internal exhaust system.

Standard at shows and events with an audience. It can be positioned 10-15 m from the stage without compromising the event's sound.

Emergency Generator (UPS + Diesel)

System with a battery UPS coupled to a diesel generator. Automatic switchover in under 20 ms in the event of a utility failure. Power: 10 to 500 kVA.

High-end corporate events where any interruption is unacceptable. It protects servers, emergency lighting and critical systems.

Trailer Generator

Generator mounted on a 2- or 4-axle trailer. Integrated fuel tank for 8 to 24 hours of autonomy. Easy positioning and transport. Power: 100 to 800 kVA.

Events in remote locations or without easy truck access. Common at open-air festivals and touring events.

Redundant Generator Set

Two generators in parallel with an automatic transfer switch (ATS). One generator operates, the other stays on hot standby.

Large shows where a failure of the main generator cannot interrupt the event. High cost, but maximum reliability.


HOW TO SIZE THE GENERATOR — LOAD CALCULATION


Sizing the generator wrong is the most common and most expensive electrical mistake at events. An undersized generator will sag in voltage and frequency when the load increases — and that destroys amplifiers, computers and lighting systems. The rule: always size with a 30% margin over the total calculated load.


Resistive load (heaters, incandescent lamps)

Power factor (PF) = 1.0. The power consumed equals the rated power. Simple to calculate.

Inductive load (motors, transformers, trussing motors)

PF between 0.7 and 0.85. It draws more current than the apparent power indicates. It raises the starting current to 5 to 7 times the rated value.

Non-linear load (amplifiers, switching power supplies, LED drivers)

PF between 0.5 and 0.9. It generates harmonics that heat cables and transformers. It requires a generator with a high-quality AVR and low waveform distortion.

Motor starting current

A 10 kW motor can demand 50-70 kW at startup (inrush current). The generator must handle this without a voltage drop above 10%.

Practical sizing rule

Add up all the loads (kW). Divide by the average power factor (0.8). Multiply by 1.3 (30% margin). The result is the minimum generator power in kVA.

Real example: a mid-size show

PA: 60 kW + Lighting: 80 kW + Video: 20 kW + Backline: 10 kW + Catering/Infrastructure: 20 kW = 190 kW ÷ 0.8 × 1.3 = 308 kVA minimum → use a 350 kVA generator.


CORRECT GENERATOR INSTALLATION — STEP BY STEP


1. Positioning

Minimum 15 m from the stage for open generators. Silent: minimum 7 m. Never position it with the exhaust pointing toward the audience area, performers or tent ventilation. Check the wind direction.

2. Leveling

The generator must be level (±3 mm) for correct operation of the engine and alternator. Use anti-vibration rubber pads between the generator and the ground.

3. Grounding

Drive a copper-clad steel grounding rod (earth electrode) of 3/4" × 2.4 m to a minimum depth of 1.5 m. Grounding cable: minimum 16 mm² green/yellow connected to the generator's ground bus and to the distribution panel.

4. Fuel

External tank connected by a diesel-resistant hose. Allow 4 to 6 liters per hour per 100 kVA of load. An 8-hour event with a 300 kVA generator: allow 120 to 180 liters. A BC powder extinguisher positioned 2 m away.

5. Warm-up

Before connecting the load, warm up the engine for at least 3 minutes with no load. Connect the loads progressively — never all at once.

6. Shutdown

Disconnect the loads progressively. Let the engine run for 3 minutes with no load before shutting down (turbo cooling, where applicable).

7. Monitoring during the event

Check the fuel level every 2h. Monitor the engine temperature (do not exceed the value on the panel). Check the output voltage (220V ±5% = 209V to 231V) and frequency (60 Hz ±0.5 Hz).


❌  CRITICAL ERROR — NEVER DO THIS

✅  CORRECT EXECUTION

Connecting the entire load at once when energizing the generator

Connect the loads progressively — PA first, then lighting, then video

Positioning the generator exhaust pointing toward the stage

Exhaust always facing away from the stage, audience and enclosed tents

Using a generator without grounding on a wet floor

Grounding rod driven in before any load connection

Sizing the generator exactly at the calculated load (100%)

Always a 30% margin — a generator operating above 80% of its load degrades and may fail

Refueling with the generator running

Disconnect the load, wait 3 minutes, shut off the engine to refuel

Using a thin rubber extension to feed the main panel

A cable of adequate gauge (minimum 35 mm²) in direct connection to the generator's output terminal

🔌  ELECTRICAL CABLES — GAUGES, TYPES AND APPLICATIONS


The cable is the artery of the electrical system. Choosing the wrong gauge is the most dangerous mistake an event electrician can make — because the result is not immediate: the cable heats up slowly, melts the insulation, and the fire starts where no one is watching, inside a case or behind a panel.


UNDERSTANDING CABLE GAUGE — CROSS-SECTION IN MM²


A cable's gauge is measured by the cross-section of its conductors in square millimeters (mm²). The larger the gauge, the greater the capacity to carry current (ampacity) without heating up. The correct choice of gauge depends on three factors: the current that will flow, the length of the cable and the operating temperature.

CABLE TYPES AND CONSTRUCTION — WHAT IS INSIDE THE INSULATION


CABLE TYPE

CONSTRUCTION AND CHARACTERISTICS

SPECIFIC USE AT EVENTS

PP Cable (Parallel / Hose)

Tinned multi-strand copper conductor, PVC insulation, flexible PVC sheath. Cores: 2 to 4 conductors. Gauges: 1.5 to 6 mm². Temperature: 70°C.

Equipment connections, dressing-room extensions, support lighting. Not recommended for loads above 20A or distances above 15 m.

Flex Cable (Flexible NF/NFF)

Bare, highly flexible multi-strand copper conductor, EPR or XLPE insulation, neoprene rubber or PVC sheath. Gauges: 2.5 to 35 mm². Withstands repeated bending.

Distribution sub-circuits, power supply for racks and amplifiers. Withstands being stepped on when not coiled. Standard for indoor events.

Rubber Cable (RX / H07RN-F)

Tinned multi-strand copper conductor, EPDM rubber insulation, polychloroprene (neoprene) rubber sheath. High mechanical, oil and abrasion resistance. Gauges: 1.5 to 240 mm². Temperature: 60°C to +90°C.

The absolute standard for outdoor areas at events. It withstands rain, being run over, generator oil and extreme temperatures. Mandatory on stage roofs.

COBRA Cable (Rubber 95 mm² or larger)

High-section multi-strand rubber cable (70 to 240 mm²), highly flexible, reinforced rubber sheath, weight: 3 to 8 kg/meter. Outer diameter: 25 to 45 mm.

Main power supply of the system — from the generator to the Mainframe or main panel. The name 'cobra' comes from its weight, size and relative stiffness. Handling requires a 2-person team.

Dry Cable (Power — NYY/Solid)

Solid or stranded copper or aluminum conductor, PVC insulation, PVC sheath. Not flexible. Section: 1.5 to 300 mm². For fixed installations.

Do NOT use at events. Solid cable breaks with repeated bending. For use only in permanent installations.

Multipair Instrumentation Cable

Multiple pairs of low-section conductors (0.5 to 1.5 mm²) with individual and collective shielding. Electromagnetic interference (EMI) rejection.

Audio signal snake, DMX cabling for lighting, control video cabling. Not to be confused with power cable.

🔧  TERMINALS AND CONNECTIONS — THE ART OF MAKING A SAFE JOINT


The terminal is the most critical point of any temporary electrical installation. It is where cables connect to busbars, breakers and equipment. A poorly made connection creates contact resistance — and contact resistance generates heat — and heat causes fire. Most electrical fires at events begin at poorly made terminals.


TERMINAL TYPES AND THEIR CORRECT APPLICATION


TERMINAL TYPE

TECHNICAL SPECIFICATION

APPLICATION AND NOTES

Copper Tubular Compression Terminal

Electrolytic copper tube with an inspection hole. Lengths: 30 to 50 mm. For cables from 6 to 240 mm². Crimped with a hexagonal-jaw hydraulic press.

Mandatory standard for connections to copper busbars, breakers and panel connections. The hydraulic press pressure creates a cold weld that eliminates air gaps between the cable strands and the terminal.

L-Shaped Compression Terminal (Angle Lug)

Same material as the tubular one, but with the lug (ring) bent at 90° to ease attachment to a busbar. For cables from 10 to 240 mm².

Used when the fixing screw is perpendicular to the busbar. Common in professional distribution panels and generator connections.

Bimetallic Compression Terminal (Copper-Aluminum)

Aluminum tube with an internal copper lining. Required when the cable is aluminum and the busbar is copper. With internal antioxidant paste.

MANDATORY when connecting an aluminum cable to a copper busbar or equipment. Without the bimetallic terminal, the copper-aluminum galvanic pair corrodes the junction and causes severe overheating.

Fork Terminal (Spade)

Tinned copper terminal with a fork-shaped end (two prongs). For cables from 1.5 to 16 mm². Crimped with crimping pliers.

For connections to screw terminals that do not open completely. It prevents the cable from coming loose from the terminal even if the screw loosens slightly.

Ring Terminal (Eyelet)

Tinned copper terminal with a closed ring. For cables from 1.5 to 35 mm². Crimped with a specific tool.

A permanent connection on busbars with a through-bolt. The cable does not come off even if the screw loosens. Preferred for high-current connections.

Camlock Connector (Stage Pin)

Quick-connect connector with a male/female copper conductor. Standard from 16 mm² to 120 mm². Color-coded by function: black=phase, white=neutral, green=ground. Capacity: 400A (large) to 60A (small).

The international STANDARD for quick power connections at events. The female always goes on the side that carries voltage (generator OUTPUT / panel INPUT). It enables safe connection and disconnection of multiple branches.

Socapex Connector (19 Pins)

A 19-pin multipolar connector for lighting power distribution. Each group of pins carries a 16A circuit. It covers up to 6 lighting circuits in a single connector.

The backstage standard for distributing stage-lighting circuits. It reduces the number of cables from the dimmer to the lighting grid.

HOW TO MAKE A COPPER TERMINAL CORRECTLY — STEP BY STEP


STEP 1 — Measure the stripping length

The length to strip equals the internal length of the terminal's tube. Use the terminal itself to measure against the cable before cutting. Most terminals for 95 mm² have a 40-45 mm tube.

STEP 2 — Strip the cable

Use a cable stripper or a fresh-blade utility knife. Do not use pliers or a knife — risk of cutting the copper strands. Strip in a spiral, rotating the blade around the cable without applying excessive pressure.

STEP 3 — Inspect the strands

After stripping, check that no copper strand was cut. A cut strand reduces the cable's effective cross-section and increases contact resistance. If there are cut strands, cut the cable back 5 cm and start over.

STEP 4 — Apply antioxidant paste (mandatory)

Apply zinc oxide paste (electrical antioxidant paste) inside the terminal's tube and on the tip of the strands. The paste fills the spaces between the strands, expels the air and prevents future oxidation of the junction.

STEP 5 — Insert the cable into the terminal

Insert the copper strands into the terminal's tube with a rotating motion. Check through the terminal's inspection hole that the strands have reached the bottom. If the inspection hole does not show shiny copper, the cable did not go in completely.

STEP 6 — Crimp with a hydraulic press

Position the terminal in the correct hexagonal jaw (the size matching the terminal). Make 2 crimps on the tube: one 10 mm from the start and one 10 mm from the end. Check that the hexagonal marks are uniform and symmetrical.

STEP 7 — Apply heat-shrink tubing

Slide a heat-shrink tube of adequate size over the cable before crimping, position it over the terminal+cable junction and activate it with a heat gun. The tube must cover at least 20 mm of the cable and the entire length of the terminal's tube.

STEP 8 — Label and test

Label the terminal: phase (A/B/C), circuit and destination. Measure the junction's resistance with a multimeter in ohm mode — the value must be below 0.5 mΩ. A high value indicates insufficient crimping.


🔴  The most dangerous mistake — an aluminum terminal on a copper busbar without a bimetallic connector

Aluminum and copper form a galvanic pair: when in direct, moist contact, they react chemically. The aluminum oxidizes rapidly at the junction, creating an oxide layer of very high electrical resistance. This resistance generates localized heat that, over time:

1. Chars the cable insulation in the terminal region

2. Causes progressive contact failure that generates an electric arc

3. Starts a fire inside the panel or case where it is installed

SOLUTION: Always use a bimetallic copper-aluminum terminal with antioxidant paste when the cable is aluminum. NEVER connect an aluminum cable directly to a copper busbar or terminal.


CAMLOCK CONNECTORS — THE EVENT STANDARD


The Camlock connector is the universal standard for quick power connections at events. Originally developed for industry, it was adopted by the entertainment sector for its robustness, current capacity and intrinsic safety — the design prevents accidental connection of phase to neutral.


CAMLOCK TYPE

CAPACITY AND SIZE

IDENTIFICATION AND STANDARD

Camlock 400A (Large)

400A @ 600V. Diameter: 57 mm (male) / 60 mm (female). For cables from 70 to 240 mm². Polypropylene or aluminum body.

Black = Phase. White = Neutral. Green = Ground. Red and Blue = Phases B and C in three-phase systems. NEMA/IEC standard.

Camlock 200A (Medium)

200A @ 600V. Diameter: 38 mm (male) / 41 mm (female). For cables from 16 to 70 mm².

Same color coding as the 400A. Common in distributing mid-size PA and lighting circuits.

Camlock 60A (Small / Bates)

60A @ 600V. Diameter: 19 mm. For cables from 4 to 16 mm². Also called 'Stage Pin' or 'Bates Connector'.

A North American standard widely used in stage lighting and effects power supply. Three pins: phase, neutral, ground.

Fundamental Camlock rule

The FEMALE always goes on the side that carries voltage — the generator output, the panel output. The MALE always goes on the receiving side — the panel input, the equipment input.

This convention ensures that the energized contact (female) is covered and protected when disconnected, reducing the risk of accidental shock.

🗄️  DISTRIBUTION PANELS — MAINFRAME, DIMMER AND SUB-PANELS


The distribution panel is the brain of the event's electrical infrastructure. It is where the generator's raw power is protected, metered, distributed and controlled for each department of the event. A well-assembled and correctly sized panel is invisible during the show — everything simply works.


MAINFRAME (MAIN PANEL) — MANDATORY COMPONENTS


Three-phase copper busbar

High-conductivity electrolytic copper busbar (99.9% Cu). Sized for the total system current. Connects the generator input to all the output breakers.

Main breaker (RCD + SPD)

Three-phase thermomagnetic main breaker with a trip current matched to the generator. Accompanied by a Surge Protection Device (SPD) to protect the equipment from voltage spikes.

Individual output breakers

One thermomagnetic breaker per output circuit. Trip current set according to the load and the cable gauge. Never overload breakers with loads exceeding their rated current.

Residual Current Device (RCD)

Protects against leakage currents (electric shocks). Sensitivity of 30 mA for circuits used by people. Trips in under 30 ms — enough to prevent cardiac fibrillation.

Voltage and current meters (voltmeter / ammeter)

Panel voltmeter for continuous monitoring of the generator voltage (must maintain 220V ±5%). Ammeter per phase to check load balancing.

Ground busbar (PE)

Ground busbar separate from the neutral, connected to the grounding electrode. A single ground reference point for the whole system — it prevents potential differences between equipment.

Neutral busbar (N)

Neutral busbar isolated from the ground. In TN-S systems (the event standard), neutral and ground are separate throughout the system — do not connect them at any point except at the generator.

Input connector (female Camlock)

Female 400A Camlock inputs (black, red, blue, white, green) to receive the cables coming from the generator.

Output connector (female Camlock)

Female Camlock outputs per circuit — PA, lighting, video, backline, infrastructure. Each output protected by its individual breaker.

Metal enclosure with adequate IP rating

For outdoor events: a panel with a minimum IP55 rating (protection against dust and water jets). For indoor: IP44. Never use plastic construction boxes in systems above 63A.

PHASE BALANCING — A TECHNICAL OBLIGATION FREQUENTLY IGNORED


A three-phase generator has three phases (A, B, C) that must carry approximately equal currents. When the loads are unbalanced — for example, all the PA on one phase and all the lighting on another — the generator suffers, the neutral heats up, and the voltage of one phase may rise or fall, compromising the equipment.


⚡  Golden rule of three-phase balancing

The current difference between the three phases must be less than 10% of the total current. Before energizing, calculate the load of each phase and redistribute the circuits if necessary:

Phase A: PA Main (60A) + Infra 1 (20A) = 80A

Phase B: Lighting Main (55A) + Backline (25A) = 80A

Phase C: Video (40A) + Catering (30A) + Infra 2 (10A) = 80A

Maximum difference: 0A = perfect balancing. Result: stable generator, cool neutral, constant voltage.


GROUNDING AT EVENTS — WHAT MOST PEOPLE IGNORE


Grounding at events is not optional and is not just 'sticking a pin in the socket'. In temporary installations, correct grounding is the only effective protection against electric shocks caused by insulation failures — and it is also what ensures the audio equipment does not produce ground noise (ground loop).


Grounding electrode (rod)

Copper-clad steel rod of 3/4" × 2.4 m or 5/8" × 1.5 m. Driven vertically into moist soil to a minimum depth of 1.5 m. In dry or rocky soils, use a ring (perimeter) electrode with a buried 35 mm² cable.

Main grounding cable

Minimum section of 50% of the phase cable for three-phase systems. For a 95 mm² cobra: grounding of at least 50 mm². Green-yellow cable, without a joint, from the generator to the panel's ground busbar.

Acceptable ground resistance

Maximum 10 Ω for temporary installations (NBR 5410). Measure with an earth resistance tester before energizing. Very dry soil can have high resistance — moistening around the rod improves the contact.

Ground loop (ground noise in audio)

It occurs when two audio devices have ground connections of different resistance — creating a potential difference that appears as 60 Hz noise in the amplifiers. Solution: all audio and lighting equipment on the same ground busbar (Single Point Ground).

Potential equalization (bonding)

Connect the metal housings of all equipment to the ground busbar, including trusses, the metal stage and protective barriers. It prevents a potential difference that could cause a shock when touching two pieces of equipment simultaneously.

VOLTAGE DROP AS A FUNCTION OF LENGTH — SIMPLIFIED CALCULATION


The voltage drop along a cable is proportional to the length and the current, and inversely proportional to the cable's cross-section. A voltage drop above 3% in the power cables unbalances the loads, reduces the amplifiers' power and can damage sensitive equipment.


📐  Voltage-drop formula (simplified DC for single-phase AC)

ΔV (%) = (2 × L × I × ρ) / (S × Vn) × 100


Where:

L = cable length in meters (one direction)

I = current in amperes

ρ = resistivity of copper = 0.0175 Ω·mm²/m

S = cable cross-section in mm²

Vn = nominal voltage (220V)


Example: 95 mm², 50 m of cable, 150A of current:

ΔV = (2 × 50 × 150 × 0.0175) / (95 × 220) × 100 = 262.5 / 20900 × 100 = 1.26% ✅ (below 3%)


If the cable were 35 mm² in the same case:

ΔV = (2 × 50 × 150 × 0.0175) / (35 × 220) × 100 = 262.5 / 7700 × 100 = 3.41% ❌ (above 3%)

⚠️  CRITICAL ERRORS IN EVENT ELECTRICAL WORK — AND HOW TO AVOID THEM


The errors below are real, frequent and potentially fatal. Each one has already caused fires, damage to high-value equipment or electric shocks at events in Brazil and worldwide. Learn to recognize them before you witness them.


❌  CRITICAL ERROR — NEVER DO THIS

✅  CORRECT EXECUTION

Using ordinary electrical tape instead of terminals on power cables above 16 mm²

Always finish with a copper compression terminal + hydraulic press. Electrical tape is not a terminal.

Connecting an aluminum cable directly to a copper busbar or terminal

Use a bimetallic copper-aluminum terminal with antioxidant paste — it eliminates the corrosive galvanic pair.

Coiling a thin 2.5 mm² PP cable into an improvised extension to feed a 5 kW amplifier

Calculate the equipment current and size the cable correctly. 5kW @ 220V = 22.7A → minimum 4 mm² cable.

Placing the male Camlock on the generator output side (the hot side)

The FEMALE always goes on the hot side. The male goes on the side that receives power.

Using a 100A breaker to protect a 10 mm² cable (limit 46A)

The breaker MUST be equal to or smaller than the cable's ampacity. A larger breaker does not protect the cable — only the equipment.

Not grounding because the generator 'already has an internal ground'

Drive an external ground electrode and measure the resistance before energizing — even with a modern generator.

Leaving cable joints along the route covered by a mat or road case

Joints must be in sealed, accessible junction boxes. A joint under weight heats up and catches fire.

Using a household reel extension coiled up to feed a 3 kW load

A coiled cable does not dissipate heat — it must be fully unwound before energizing.

Not balancing the phases — putting all the PA on phase A and all the lighting on phase B

Distribute the loads among the three phases with a maximum difference of 10% between them.

Connecting the neutral to the ground busbar at some point after the generator (an improvised TN-C system)

Neutral and ground separate throughout the system after the generator. Bonding only at the generator's grounding point.

Using the stage's metal chassis as the neutral return conductor

The neutral must follow the same route as the phase cables. Never use a metal structure as a conductor.

Energizing the system without checking whether someone is working on the installation (without LOTO)

Apply LOTO (lockout and tagout) before any maintenance. Check by radio with everyone before energizing.

✅  COMPLETE ELECTRICAL CHECKLIST — PRE-ENERGIZATION AND OPERATION


Use this checklist before energizing any event electrical system. One unchecked item could be the start of the next fire.


PRE-ENERGIZATION — BEFORE SWITCHING ON ANY BREAKER


Generator leveled and secured

Check the physical level and that the anti-vibration pads are in place.

Ground electrode installed

Rod driven in, ground cable connected to the generator and the panel. Measured resistance: <10 Ω.

All terminals inspected

Check the terminal on each cable: correct crimping, heat-shrink tube applied, no slack at the terminal.

Cables secured and protected

Cables on the ground: covered with a cable ramp. Aerial cables: fixed with cable ties every 1.5 m.

Phase and polarity verified

Check with a phase meter that the A/B/C phases of the Camlocks match the phases of the generator and the panel.

Phase balancing calculated

Add up the loads per phase. Maximum 10% difference between the phases. Redistribute if necessary.

All breakers off

All output breakers of the main panel in the OFF position before energizing the generator.

LOTO released

Confirm by radio with all departments that no one is working on the installation.

Fuel checked

Fuel level above 75% for the autonomy of the entire event.

Extinguisher positioned

A BC powder extinguisher of at least 4 kg, no more than 2 m from the generator and the main panel.


ENERGIZATION — CORRECT SEQUENCE


STEP 1

Start the generator with no load (all breakers OFF). Wait 3 minutes for warm-up.

STEP 2

Check the output voltage on each phase (220V ±5%) and the frequency (60.0 Hz ±0.5 Hz) on the generator panel.

STEP 3

Switch on the main breaker of the main panel. Check the voltage on the output busbars again.

STEP 4

Switch on the output breakers ONE AT A TIME, in the order: infrastructure → audiovisual → lighting → PA.

STEP 5

After each breaker, check the ammeter per phase. Confirm that the current is within the expected range.

STEP 6

At full load, check: voltage (220V ±5%), frequency (60 Hz ±0.5 Hz), generator temperature (normal).


MONITORING DURING THE EVENT


Every 30 minutes

Check the ammeters per phase. Any sudden variation above 20A = investigate immediately.

Every 1 hour

Check the generator's output voltage. A voltage drop may indicate overload or a problem with the AVR.

Every 2 hours

Check the fuel level, engine temperature and the temperature of the main cables (a quick touch — it should not burn).

At any time

Smell of burning rubber = interrupt the event, identify the overheated cable or terminal before any other action.

In case of a power outage

Do not restart automatically. Identify the cause (overload, short circuit, generator failure) before energizing again.


🎯  Conclusion — Energy is power. Power poorly controlled is danger.

An event's electrical system is invisible when it works correctly — and catastrophic when it fails. The event electrician who masters what was presented in this lesson is a rare and valuable professional: they understand the system as a whole, size it safely, execute it with precision and monitor it attentively.


Every cable chosen for the correct gauge, every terminal crimped with a hydraulic press, every balanced phase, every measured ground electrode — all of these are decisions that protect the lives of the performers, the crew and the audience. It is not technical bureaucracy. It is professional responsibility.


🏅  The 7 commandments of event electrical work

1. Never work on electrical installations without NR-10 qualification — not even 'just to help'.

2. Always size the generator with a 30% margin — a suffocating generator is a generator that will fail.

3. Always use a compression terminal with a hydraulic press — electrical tape is not a terminal.

4. Never connect aluminum to copper without a bimetallic terminal with antioxidant paste.

5. The female Camlock always goes on the hot side — never on the side that receives power.

6. Balance the phases before energizing — a maximum difference of 10% between them.

7. Drive a ground electrode and measure the resistance before any energization — no exceptions.



"The event electrician does not see the result of their work on stage.

They feel it — because the stage simply works."

SMU PRODUÇÕES

Professional Events School

Basic Electronics for Events

Signals, Connectors, Cables, Measurement and Diagnosis

"Whoever understands the signal understands the problem.

Whoever understands the problem solves it before the show starts."

📋  Why basic electronics is essential at events

At professional events, high-value electronic equipment operates 24 hours straight, in adverse environments — dust, humidity, vibration, temperature variation — connected by hundreds of meters of cables running under road cases, behind trusses and along wet floors. The inevitable result is: something will always go wrong.


The technician who understands basic electronics — who knows how to measure voltage, identify a broken cable by continuity, understand the difference between balanced and unbalanced signal, know why there is a 60 Hz hum in the artist's monitor — is the one who solves the problem in the 3 minutes before the show starts, while everyone else is panicking.


This lesson covers the essentials that any events technician needs to master: electrical quantities, applied Ohm's law, audio and data signals, all the connectors used at events, how to make a good solder joint, how to use the multimeter and how to diagnose common faults. Without complicated formulas — with direct practical applications.


⚡  Basic electronics is not optional for events technicians

Every sound, video and lighting technician will, at some point, come across a broken cable, a connector with a bad contact, an inexplicable noise or a piece of equipment that won't turn on. Those who don't know basic electronics call a technician from another department to solve a simple problem. Those who do solve it in minutes — and don't stop the show.

⚡  ELECTRICAL QUANTITIES — WHAT YOU NEED TO UNDERSTAND


Before any connector or equipment, you need to understand the fundamental electrical quantities. Not as abstract theory — as a practical tool you will use every time you pick up a multimeter.


VOLTAGE, CURRENT, RESISTANCE AND POWER


Voltage (V — Volt)

It is the 'pressure' that pushes the electrons through the circuit. Like water pressure in a pipe. Without voltage, there is no current. At events: 220V on the power, +48V on the phantom power, ±15V in equipment power supplies, 3.3V and 5V in digital circuits.

Current (A — Ampere)

It is the flow of electrons circulating through the circuit. Like the volume of water passing through the pipe. At events: dozens of amperes in power cables, milliamperes in audio signal cables.

Resistance (Ω — Ohm)

It is the opposition to the flow of current. Every conductive material has resistance. At events: contact resistance in poorly made terminals causes heating; wrong audio cable resistance causes signal loss.

Power (W — Watt)

It is the energy consumed per second. P = V × I. At events: 1,000W amplifiers, 500W lighting per fixture, 250,000W (250 kW) generator. Understanding power means knowing how to size cables and circuit breakers.

Frequency (Hz — Hertz)

It is the number of cycles per second in alternating current (AC). In Brazil: 60 Hz. In audio: frequency is what defines the pitch (20 Hz = deep bass, 20,000 Hz = extreme treble). The human ear hears from 20 Hz to 20 kHz.

Decibel (dB)

Logarithmic unit of signal level. In audio: dBu (voltage), dBV (voltage ref. 1V), dBFS (digital full scale), dBSPL (sound pressure). +6dB = doubling the voltage. +20dB = 10x the voltage. -3dB = reducing the power by half.


OHM'S LAW — THE RULE OF EVERYTHING


Ohm's Law describes the relationship between voltage, current and resistance in any electrical circuit. It is the tool most used by the events technician, even without realizing they are applying it.


📐  Ohm's Law — the three forms and practical applications

V = R × I     (voltage = resistance × current)

I = V / R     (current = voltage / resistance)

R = V / I     (resistance = voltage / current)


Application 1 — Undersized cable:

A 2.5mm² cable has a resistance of 7.4 Ω/km. Over 50m: 0.37 Ω total.

With 20A flowing: voltage drop = 0.37 × 20 = 7.4V (3.4% drop at 220V — acceptable)


Application 2 — Terminal with contact resistance:

An oxidized terminal has 0.5 Ω. With 20A: P = I² × R = 400 × 0.5 = 200W generated at the terminal.

200W concentrated at a 1cm² point = fire.


Application 3 — Phantom power:

A condenser microphone needs +48V. Internal resistance of 6.8 kΩ per conductor.

Current: I = 48 / 6800 = 7mA. Power: P = 48 × 0.007 = 0.34W. Safe for the XLR cable.


DIRECT CURRENT (DC) AND ALTERNATING CURRENT (AC)


TYPE

CHARACTERISTIC

USE AT EVENTS

Direct Current (DC)

Constant flow in one direction. Constant voltage over time. Produced by batteries, DC power supplies and switching power supplies.

Internal power supply of amplifiers, mixing consoles, computers, LEDs, processors. Phantom power (+48V DC) for condenser microphones.

Alternating Current (AC)

Flow that reverses direction 60 times per second (60 Hz in Brazil). Sinusoidal waveform. Produced by generators and utility companies.

Power for the entire power infrastructure of the event. What comes out of the generator, the panel and the outlets is AC. Amplifiers convert AC to DC internally via the power supply.

Phantom Power (+48V DC)

DC voltage of +48V applied through pins 2 and 3 of the XLR simultaneously, with reference to pin 1. Maximum current: 14 mA. IEC 61938 standard.

Powers condenser microphones and active DIs directly through the XLR cable. Turns on/off at the mixing console. Does not damage dynamic microphones, but can damage ribbons — check before turning on.

Audio Signal (audio AC)

Low-amplitude alternating voltage that represents sound. Frequency from 20 Hz to 20 kHz. Amplitude: from -60 dBu (quiet microphone) to +24 dBu (power amp output).

The signal that travels through XLR, TRS, RCA cables from the microphone to the amplifier. Each stage of the system processes, amplifies or routes this signal.

🔈  AUDIO SIGNALS — BALANCED AND UNBALANCED


Understanding the difference between balanced and unbalanced signal is one of the most practical skills an events technician can have. Most of the noise, hum and interference that appears in audio systems at events originates from incorrect connections between balanced and unbalanced equipment.


UNBALANCED SIGNAL


The unbalanced signal uses two conductors: a signal conductor (hot) and a reference/shield conductor (ground). The signal is transmitted as a potential difference between the two. Any electromagnetic interference picked up by the cable enters directly into the signal.


Conductors

2: signal (hot) + shield/ground.

Typical impedance

10 kΩ (instrument input), 50 kΩ (guitar amplifier input).

Maximum practical length

3 to 5 meters. Beyond that, noise and high-frequency loss become audible.

Typical connectors

TS (6.35mm mono plug), RCA (phono), 3.5mm TS (mono mini-jack), instrument cable.

Where it is used

Guitar → pedal → amplifier. Passive DI output to the console. Connection of mobile device/laptop to the console.

Main problem

Any nearby electromagnetic field (parallel power cable, light dimmer, motor) induces noise directly into the signal. The noise is NOT canceled.

BALANCED SIGNAL


The balanced signal uses three conductors: hot (+), cold (–) and shield. The same signal is transmitted on both conductors, but with inverted polarity on the cold. On arrival, the receiver sums hot with the inversion of cold — canceling any noise that was picked up equally on both conductors. This is the principle of Common Mode Rejection (CMRR).


Conductors

3: hot (+), cold (–), shield/ground. Can be a 2-pair cable (star quad) for greater CMRR.

CMRR (Common Mode Rejection Ratio)

Ability to reject noise picked up equally on both conductors. Good mixing console: CMRR of 80 dB or more — rejects 99.99% of the noise.

Practical length

100 to 300 meters without significant degradation with good quality cable. 100m snakes are common in large shows.

Typical connectors

XLR3 (universal standard), 6.35mm TRS (balanced), etherCON (Dante/AES67), AES/EBU (balanced digital XLR).

Where it is used

Microphone → snake → mixing console. Console output → amplifier. Processor send/return. Any signal that travels more than 5 meters in a show environment.

Practical advantage

In a show with 200 light dimmers generating interference, 50-meter balanced cables from the stage to the FOH may have no audible noise. Unbalanced cables would have a constant hum.


STAR QUAD — THE HIGH-PERFORMANCE BALANCED CABLE


The Star Quad cable (such as the Canare L-4E6S and Mogami W2534) has four conductors instead of two, arranged in an X. The opposite conductors are connected together (1+3 = hot, 2+4 = cold). This geometry increases the CMRR by up to 20 dB compared to the 2-conductor cable — making it ideal for high-interference environments such as stages with dimmers and generators.


CABLE

CONSTRUCTION

IDEAL USE

Canare L-4E6S (Star Quad)

4 conductors of 0.22mm² each, twisted pair, 97% shield (copper spiral), diameter 6.5mm. Capacitance: 51 pF/m.

Microphones on stages with high dimmer interference. Stage snake. Any long-distance application in a noisy environment.

Mogami W2534 (Star Quad)

4 conductors of 0.20mm², 95% copper spiral shield, diameter 6.7mm. World reference in signal quality.

Microphones for lead vocalists, DI for main instruments. The cable that artist riders most demand by name.

Mogami W2549 (Mic Cable)

2 twisted conductors of 0.22mm², double shield (spiral + serve). Ultra-flexible. For microphones.

Wireless microphones, short high-quality stage cables.

Belden 1800F (Mic/Line)

2 conductors 24AWG, aluminum shield+drain, diameter 5.5mm. Widely used in fixed installations.

Patch bay installations, rack connections, fixed installation snake in theater and TV.

Generic event cable (PP 2×0.5mm²)

2 conductors of 0.5mm² in plain PVC, minimal or no shield.

Do not use in a professional show environment. Very high noise pickup, low durability. For bench testing only.

🔌  CONNECTORS — EACH ONE, ITS FUNCTION AND CORRECT PINOUT


Connectors are the junction points of an event's electronic system. Each type has a specific function — and confusing them is one of the most common sources of problems. Knowing each connector, its pinout and its correct application is a basic competency of every technician.


ANALOG AUDIO CONNECTORS


CONNECTOR

PINS / CONFIGURATION

SIGNAL / USE

TECHNICAL NOTE

XLR 3 Pin (Male/Female)

Pin 1: Ground/Shield. Pin 2: Hot (+). Pin 3: Cold (–).

Microphone signal, balanced line signal, console return to amplifier.

Universal professional audio standard. Male = signal output. Female = signal input. NEVER swap pins 2 and 3 — it creates a phase inversion that cancels the sound in multichannel systems.

XLR 5 Pin (Male/Female)

Pins 1-3: same as XLR3. Pins 4-5: second balanced pair.

Microphone cable with integrated lighting (followspot). Balanced stereo microphones.

Less common. Professional followspots use XLR5 to carry intercom signal along with the fixture's power cable.

TRS 6.35mm (Stereo/Balanced)

Tip (T): hot (+) / left channel. Ring (R): cold (–) / right channel. Sleeve (S): ground.

Balanced mono (Tip=hot, Ring=cold, Sleeve=ground) OR unbalanced stereo (Tip=L, Ring=R, Sleeve=GND).

Processor insert on the console: Tip=send, Ring=return, Sleeve=GND. Balanced output of equipment without XLR. Stereo headphones.

TS 6.35mm (Mono/Instrument)

Tip (T): signal. Sleeve (S): ground.

Guitar → pedals, instrument → passive DI. Unbalanced signal.

Never use in place of balanced TRS. When inserting TS into a TRS input, the cold gets connected to ground — it opens the balanced circuit and loses CMRR.

RCA / Phono

Center: positive signal. Outer ring: ground.

CD player output, DJ mixer output, hi-fi amplifier input.

Unbalanced line-level signal. Common in consumer equipment. For professional use, always use an RCA→XLR adapter with an active or passive DI.

3.5mm / Mini-Jack (TRS/TS)

TRS: Tip=L, Ring=R, Sleeve=GND (stereo). TS: Tip=signal, Sleeve=GND (mono).

Output of smartphone, laptop, tablet, photo camera.

To use on a professional console: adapt with a 3.5mm TRS → 2× TS (Y-cable) or 3.5mm TRS → XLR with active DI. Never connect directly to an XLR input without adaptation.


DIGITAL AUDIO AND DATA CONNECTORS


CONNECTOR

PROTOCOL / STANDARD

SIGNAL / USE

TECHNICAL NOTE

AES/EBU (XLR3)

AES3. Impedance: 110 Ω. Sample rate: up to 192 kHz.

Digital audio transmission between professional equipment — mixing console to D/A converter, processor to digital amplifier.

Same physical connector as the analog XLR, but incompatible protocol. Use a specific 110 Ω cable for AES — an analog microphone cable (75 Ω) causes reflection and bit errors over distances above 30m.

S/PDIF (RCA or TOSLINK)

IEC 60958. Impedance: 75 Ω (coaxial). Optical (Toslink): no EM interference.

Digital connection between consumer and semi-professional equipment — D/A converters, sound cards, effects.

Coaxial S/PDIF: 75 Ω cable (same as video). Toslink: optical fiber, maximum distance 10m. Supports up to 2 channels (stereo).

etherCON (reinforced RJ45)

Dante, AES67, AVB, MADI over IP. Protocol: Ethernet 100/1000 Mbps.

Audio transport over IP network — up to 512 bidirectional channels on the same Cat6 cable. Connection of mixing console to Dante stage box.

The etherCON is the standard RJ45 with a reinforced Neutrik housing for stage use. It can be connected to any Ethernet switch — the audio protocol (Dante, AES67) runs at the software layer.

MADI (BNC / Optical Fiber)

AES10. Up to 64 channels on a single coaxial cable (BNC) or fiber. Distance: 50m coaxial, 2km fiber.

Long-distance digital snake — stage console to FOH in large shows. Widely used in television.

Ultra-low latency (less than 1 sample). A single 75 Ω BNC coaxial cable carries 64 channels of professional audio at 24 bits/48 kHz.

USB-A/B/C

USB 2.0: 480 Mbps. USB 3.0: 5 Gbps. USB-C: up to 40 Gbps (USB4).

Connection of USB audio interfaces to computers for recording, show playback and timecode. MIDI controllers.

USB is not designed for long-distance audio. Maximum 5m (USB 2.0) without an active hub. At shows, use a USB extender over Cat6 for greater distances.

MIDI (5-pin DIN / USB / TRS)

MIDI 1.0: 31,250 bps serial. MIDI 2.0 (new): higher resolution and bidirectional.

Control of synthesizers, samplers, stage controllers, timecode systems, triggering of special effects.

Active pins on DIN5: pin 2=GND, pin 4=+5V (current), pin 5=data. Pins 1 and 3 are not used. Maximum cable: 15m (MIDI 1.0).
