🔧  PROFESSIONAL SOLDERING — THE SKILL THAT SOLVES ANY CONNECTOR


Knowing how to solder an XLR connector correctly is the most practical and valued skill in event technicians. A microphone cable that breaks in the dressing room 20 minutes before the show starts is a problem that any technician with a soldering iron and skill solves in 5 minutes. Without that skill, the show can stop.


ESSENTIAL TOOLS FOR QUALITY SOLDERING


Soldering iron with controllable temperature

Ideal temperature for soldering audio connectors: 320°C to 360°C. Too low a temperature creates a cold solder joint (dull, grainy). Too high a temperature burns the insulation and oxidizes the pin. Hakko FX-888D or Weller WE1010 soldering stations are the market standards.

Wire solder (rosin core)

Composition: tin (Sn) + silver (Ag) + copper (Cu) or Sn60Pb40 (with lead). Diameter: 0.5mm to 1mm for audio connectors. The rosin flux inside the solder wire cleans the surface during soldering — essential for good adhesion.

Desoldering braid or suction pump

To remove old solder from pins with bad contact. The braid (wick) is placed over the hot solder and sucks it up by capillary action. The pump aspirates the molten solder.

Helping hand

Alligator-clip stand that holds the connector while you solder. Hands free to hold the iron and the solder wire simultaneously. Indispensable for soldering XLR with precision.

Wire stripper

To remove the cable insulation without damaging the conductors. Never use a utility knife or blade — it cuts the copper strands and increases the contact resistance.

Multimeter

To test the cable after soldering: continuity on each pin and absence of a short between pins. The continuity test should ALWAYS be done before using the cable.


HOW TO SOLDER AN XLR3 CORRECTLY — STEP BY STEP


STEP 1 — Prepare the cable

Strip 5cm of the outer jacket. Separate the conductors. In star quad: separate the 4 wires (2 red = hot, 2 black = cold) + drain wire (ground). Twist the pairs: red+red, black+black. Lightly tin each twisted pair.

STEP 2 — Slide on the connector's rear housing

Before any soldering, slide the connector's housing/strain relief onto the cable. It is humiliating to finish the soldering and realize you forgot. It is the most common mistake of those who are learning.

STEP 3 — Prepare the pins

Heat each pin of the connector with the iron for 2 seconds. Apply a small amount of solder to the heated pin (pre-tinning). The pin should absorb the solder and shine uniformly.

STEP 4 — Identify the pinout

XLR3: Pin 1 = Ground (drain wire + shield). Pin 2 = Hot (red / + / positive phase). Pin 3 = Cold (black / – / negative phase). Confirm with the physical markings on the connector.

STEP 5 — Solder each pin

Position the conductor over the pre-tinned pin. Heat the joint with the iron (not the solder wire — the joint). Apply the solder wire to the heated joint — the solder flows by capillary action. Remove the iron. Wait 5 seconds without moving the cable.

STEP 6 — Inspect the solder

Good solder: shiny, smooth, conical, adhered to the pin and to the wire. Cold solder (bad): dull, grainy, rounded — redo it. Excessive solder: a blob that can create a short with the neighboring pin — remove with braid.

STEP 7 — Close the strain relief

Position the cable in the connector's strain relief and tighten the screw. The strain relief holds the cable — without it, any tug goes straight to the solder.

STEP 8 — Test with a multimeter

Continuity mode: test pin 1→ground wire, pin 2→red wire, pin 3→black wire. Then: test that there is NO continuity between pins 1 and 2, 1 and 3, 2 and 3. Any short = redo it.


🔴  The 4 most common mistakes when soldering connectors

1. COLD SOLDER: cold iron or insufficient contact time. Result: intermittent contact that creates clicks and noise.

2. FORGETTING THE REAR HOUSING: noticing after soldering. Result: disassemble everything and start over.

3. SWAPPING PINS 2 AND 3: inverted phase. Result: signal cancellation in a stereo or mono system when summed.

4. EXCESSIVE SOLDER CREATING A SHORT: excess tin between adjacent pins. Result: noisy signal, phantom power with leakage current.

📊  MULTIMETER AND DIAGNOSIS — SOLVE BEFORE THE SHOW STARTS


The multimeter is the most important instrument in any events technician's backpack. With it you measure voltage, test cable continuity, identify shorts and diagnose equipment faults — before the problem becomes a crisis during the show.


MULTIMETER FUNCTIONS AND HOW TO USE THEM AT EVENTS


FUNCTION

HOW TO MEASURE

DIRECT APPLICATION AT EVENTS

AC Voltage (VAC)

Selector on VAC (or ~V). Probes on the two points. Not reversible (AC has no polarity).

Check whether the outlet is delivering 220V before turning on the amplifier. Check whether the generator is stable (must maintain 220V ±5% = 209V to 231V).

DC Voltage (VDC)

Selector on VDC (or = V). Red probe on +, black on –. Polarity matters.

Check phantom power (+48V on pins 2 and 3 of the XLR vs pin 1). Check the supply voltage of a rack power supply (±15V, ±18V). Check the battery of a wireless microphone (1.5V or 1.2V).

Continuity (Beep)

Selector on continuity (diode or bell symbol). Probes on the two ends of the cable. Beep = conducts.

Test whether an XLR cable is intact from end to end (pin 1 to pin 1, 2 to 2, 3 to 3). Identify an internally broken cable without opening it. Check whether a speakON is connected correctly.

Resistance (Ω)

Selector on Ω. Probes on the two points. The circuit must be off.

Measure the resistance of a speaker (woofer: 4Ω or 8Ω nominal — measure DC: 3.2Ω or 6.4Ω). Check whether an instrument cable is shorted (it should read very high resistance = ∞ between hot and ground with the plug disconnected from the instrument).

AC Current (ACA)

Selector on ACA. Probes in series with the circuit (ATTENTION: probe in the 10A or mA connector according to the expected current).

Check the current consumed by a piece of equipment to confirm it is within specification. Identify an overload on a circuit.

Diode (diode test)

Selector on diode mode. Probes. The display shows the diode's voltage drop (0.4V to 0.7V for Si).

Check protection diodes in power supplies. Identify a shorted diode (0V = short) or an open one (no reading). Basic diagnosis of equipment power supplies.


DIAGNOSIS OF COMMON FAULTS — WHAT TO LISTEN FOR AND WHAT TO MEASURE


Each type of problem in audio or electrical has a specific sonic or visual signature. Learning to recognize these patterns is what allows you to diagnose without going around opening each piece of equipment.


SYMPTOM

MOST LIKELY CAUSE

DIAGNOSIS AND SOLUTION

60 Hz hum in monitor or PA

Ground loop: two pieces of equipment with a potential difference at the ground. Or an unbalanced cable in an environment with interference.

Identify the equipment that, when disconnected, eliminates the hum. Use an active DI with ground lift. Check whether all equipment is at the same ground point. Replace the unbalanced cable with a balanced one.

High-frequency hiss (120 Hz buzz)

Light dimmer with phase control near the audio cables. PWM interference (LED driver).

Physically separate the audio cables from the DMX and lighting power cables. Minimum of 30cm separation or crossing at 90°. Use a star quad cable on the affected microphone.

Intermittent signal cutout (click/dropout)

Cable with an internally broken wire but intact insulation. Bad contact in a connector.

Test continuity with a multimeter while moving the cable. Fluctuating resistance = broken wire. Locate and redo the solder or replace the cable.

No signal on a specific console channel

Bad cable, connector with bad contact, console channel with a defective preamplifier, or gain at zero.

Use a known-good cable to isolate the problem: if signal appears = bad cable. If not = console problem. Check the channel gain and phantom power if it is a condenser.

Speaker distorting even with a clean signal

Clipped amplifier (excessive gain), damaged speaker (rubbing voice coil), or incorrect impedance (4Ω instead of 8Ω = amp overload).

Reduce the gain on the amplifier. Measure the DC resistance of the woofer with a multimeter (it should be ~75% of the nominal impedance). Check the amplifier's impedance configuration.

Microphone with no signal but phantom on

Condenser microphone with an internal fault. XLR cable with pins 2 and 3 swapped (cancels the phantom). Connector with pin 1 disconnected.

Test with another microphone on the same channel. Test with another cable. Measure +48V between pin 2 and pin 1 of the cable's connector with a multimeter.

Equipment won't turn on but the outlet voltage is ok

Internal fuse blown. Internal circuit breaker tripped. Internal power supply defective.

Check and replace the internal fuse (the specification is on the equipment's label). Check whether there is a reset button for the internal circuit breaker (common in Crown and QSC amplifiers). Test with another power supply if it is a rack unit with an external supply.

Constant white or pink noise on the channel

Console channel preamplifier with a high background noise. Cable with a broken shield. RF interference from a nearby wireless microphone.

Check the channel's noise floor with a terminated input (150Ω resistor on the XLR). Isolate the channel. Check whether the wireless receiver is on a free frequency.


📦  DI BOX, TRANSFORMERS AND INTERFACE EQUIPMENT


The DI (Direct Injection) box is one of the simplest and most indispensable pieces of equipment at events. It solves the fundamental problem of connecting high-impedance unbalanced signal sources (instruments, laptops, CD players) to the low-impedance balanced inputs of professional mixing consoles — without noise and without signal loss.


TYPES OF DI AND WHEN TO USE EACH ONE


TYPE OF DI

OPERATION AND SPECIFICATION

WHEN TO USE

Passive DI (Transformer)

A high-quality audio transformer (Jensen, Lundahl, Cinemag) performs the impedance conversion and the galvanic isolation. It does not need power. Transformation ratio: 12:1 typical. Attenuation: –20 dBu (pad).

Guitar/bass with high-output pickup, keyboards with line output, CD playback, audio interface output. The passive DI does not add noise — ideal for instruments with a strong signal.

Active DI (preamp circuit)

JFET or op-amp powered by phantom power (+48V) or a battery. Very high input impedance (1 MΩ+) so as not to load a guitar's piezo. Buffer gain for a weak signal. Output transformer present in quality models.

Acoustic guitar with piezo pickup (needs very high impedance). Weak-signal instrument (electric double bass, piezo violin). Laptop/notebook with a low-level 3.5mm output.

Stereo DI (2 channels)

Two DIs in a single box, can be passive or active. L and R inputs with individual XLR outputs.

Stereo keyboard, stereo laptop playback, stereo output of a DJ mixer, stereo return of a digital console.

Signal Splitter (Mult)

A passive or active signal divider that distributes one input signal to multiple independent balanced outputs with galvanic isolation between them.

Split the signal of an instrument between the amplifier on stage AND the mixing console without creating a ground loop. Indispensable for guitarists who use a live amplifier and a DI for simultaneous recording.

Re-amplifier (Reamp Box)

Inverse of the DI: converts the console's balanced signal (low impedance, line level) into a high-impedance unbalanced signal to feed instrument amplifiers.

Send the recorded double-bass signal from the console to a stage amplifier for re-amplification. Recording and playback of instruments via the console.


REFERENCE DI BRANDS AND MODELS


Radial J48 (Active DI)

World-reference active DI. Powered by +48V phantom. Jensen transformer. Input impedance: 220 kΩ. Noise: –128 dBu. Artist riders frequently demand Radial by name.

Radial JDI (Passive DI)

Passive DI with a high-quality Jensen transformer. Can work without phantom. High hum rejection. The most respected passive DI on the professional market.

Radial Pro DI (Passive)

More affordable passive DI with Radial's own transformer. Good quality for moderate budgets. Standard at medium-sized events in Brazil.

BSS AR-133 (Active DI)

Professional active DI with an integrated ground lift switch. Widely used in corporate shows and installation applications. Good value for money.

Klark Teknik DN200 (Active DI)

Active DI with professional performance at an affordable price. THD+N below 0.005%. Output transformer. Very present in Brazil due to its value for money.

Palmer PAN 04 (Passive Splitter)

Passive 3-output splitter with galvanic isolation between each output. Indispensable for splitting an instrument's signal to amp + console + recording.


⚠️  CLASSIC MISTAKES AND BEST PRACTICES IN EVENT ELECTRONICS


❌  MISTAKE — CAUSES A PROBLEM

✅  CORRECT — SOLVES THE PROBLEM

Using a 1/4" (TS) in place of speakON to connect the amplifier to the PA speaker

speakON NL4 for PA speakers. A 6.35mm TS can create a short when inserting/removing with the amplifier on.

Swapping pins 2 and 3 of the XLR — inverted phase

Always confirm: pin 1=GND, pin 2=HOT(+), pin 3=COLD(–). Use a cable tester before the show.

Connecting a ribbon microphone with phantom power (+48V) on

Turn off phantom before connecting or disconnecting any ribbon microphone. Phantom destroys ribbons instantly.

Connecting a balanced line signal (+4dBu) directly to an amplifier's instrument input

Use an active DI with a pad or inline attenuator. The instrument input is high-impedance and saturates with line level.

Soldering without sliding on the connector's rear housing first — forgetting the housing

Fit all components onto the cable before any soldering. Check the list: housing, insulator, strain relief.

Using an instrument cable (TS) for speakON in a stage monitor system

4mm² minimum speaker cable with speakON. Instrument cable has high resistance that heats up and loses power.

Measuring AC voltage with the selector on DC — wrong scale or damages the instrument

Always check the multimeter selector before touching the probes. AC on a DC selector = incorrect reading or blows the multimeter's fuse.

Leaving cables crossing power cables at a parallel angle for 5 meters

Audio cables always cross power cables at 90°. Parallel = antenna for 60 Hz hum.

Using a 3.5mm cable directly into a mixing console without a DI or adapter

3.5mm TRS → Y-cable → 2× 6.35mm TS, or 3.5mm TRS → active DI → XLR. Never directly into XLR without conversion.

Testing a cable for shorts (current mode) on the multimeter while voltage is applied

Continuity mode is only for de-energized circuits. Voltage on the multimeter in resistance mode = blows the fuse and the instrument.

📖  Technical Glossary — Terms every technician needs to master


dBu

Decibel referenced to 0.775V RMS. Professional signal level standard. 0 dBu = 0.775V. +4 dBu = professional line level. –10 dBV = semi-professional line level (equivalent to –7.78 dBu).

dBFS

Decibel Full Scale — reference for digital systems. 0 dBFS is the digital maximum (clip). The analog signal should operate between –18 dBFS and –6 dBFS for adequate headroom.

Impedance

Opposition to the flow of current in AC. Measured in Ω. Console output: 100 Ω. Console input: 10 kΩ. Rule: the output impedance should be at least 10x lower than the input impedance (bridging).

CMRR

Common Mode Rejection Ratio — the ability of a balanced circuit to reject noise picked up equally on both conductors. Expressed in dB. Professional console: 80-100 dB of CMRR.

Ground Loop

A ground loop formed when two pieces of equipment have ground references at different potentials, creating a 60 Hz current that appears as hum in the audio.

Phantom Power

+48V DC applied to pins 2 and 3 of the XLR to power condenser microphones and active DIs. Does not affect dynamic microphones. Destroys old ribbons.

Speaker Impedance

The AC resistance of the woofer, measured in Ω. 4Ω or 8Ω are standards. The amplifier must be compatible with the minimum impedance of the system — always check before turning on.

Star Quad

4-conductor cable geometry that increases the CMRR by up to 20 dB. Used in high-quality microphone cables (Canare L-4E6S, Mogami W2534).

DMX512

Digital lighting control protocol. 512 channels per universe, each channel from 0 to 255 (8 bits). Transfer rate: 250 kbps. Cable: 110 Ω XLR5 or XLR3.

Art-Net / sACN

Protocols for transporting DMX over an Ethernet network (IP). They allow multiple DMX universes on a single network cable. Art-Net: Artistic Licence standard. sACN: ANSI E1.31 standard.

Dante

Audio-over-IP network protocol (Audinate). Supports up to 512 channels on a Gigabit network. Precise clock synchronization (PTP/IEEE 1588). Used in Yamaha CL/QL/PM consoles, Shure, BSS, Lab Gruppen.

Latency

The delay between the input and the output of a digital system. In audio: measured in milliseconds or samples. Above 8ms, musicians on stage notice a difference with the monitor sound.

SNR (Signal-to-Noise Ratio)

The ratio between the level of the useful signal and the background noise. Expressed in dB. Professional console: SNR of –130 dBu or better. The higher the SNR, the cleaner the system.

THD+N

Total Harmonic Distortion + Noise. The percentage of distortion added to the signal. Professional amplifier: THD+N < 0.01%. Above 1% = clearly audible distortion.

🎯  Conclusion — Electronics is the language of the equipment

Basic electronics does not turn the events technician into an electrical engineer. It gives the professional the language to understand what the equipment is saying — and the tool to respond when it says something is wrong.


The technician who knows how to measure, diagnose, solder and identify the noise before it reaches the artist's PA is the technician the market wants. Not because knowing electronics is rare — but because it is rare to know electronics AND have the good sense to apply it calmly under pressure, at the right moment, before the show stops.


🏅  Minimum electronics kit for the events technician

• True RMS digital multimeter (Fluke 117 or similar) with continuity and diode tester

• Soldering iron with controllable temperature (Hakko FX-888D or Weller WE1010)

• Rosin-core solder wire 0.6mm Sn60Pb40

• XLR/TRS/RJ45 cable tester (SM Pro Audio CT1 or similar)

• Automatic wire stripper (for 0.5 to 6mm²)

• Assorted heat-shrink tubing (2mm, 4mm, 6mm) + heat gun

• 10 Neutrik NC3MXX male XLR3 connectors

• 10 Neutrik NC3FXX female XLR3 connectors

• 5 Neutrik NP3X 6.35mm TRS connectors

• 5 meters of Canare L-4E6S star quad cable for field repairs

• Active DI (Radial J48 or Klark Teknik DN200) for signal emergencies



"The signal does not lie.

Learn to listen to it — and you will always find the problem."


SMU Produções — Professional Events School

7edfa245-a22a-4396-a268-b79412a9e1f5.conteudo_rico:
SMU PRODUÇÕES

Professional Events School

Professional Lighting for Events

Equipment, Roles, Skills and Market


"Light doesn't illuminate the stage.

Light creates what the audience will feel."

📋  What Professional Lighting for Events Is

Event lighting isn't about turning on fixtures so people can see the stage. It's a visual language that communicates emotion, guides the audience's gaze, defines dramatic moments and transforms an ordinary space into an unforgettable experience. The Lighting Designer is a technical artist — someone who simultaneously masters the physics of light, control technology and aesthetic sensibility.


In Brazil, the stage lighting market moves hundreds of millions of reais per year. Every large-scale show carries between 200 and 2,000 light fixtures, consuming from 50 to 600 kW of power, operated by teams of 2 to 20 professionals. It's a field with high demand, a low supply of qualified professionals — and pay proportional to that scarcity.


💡  Why learn professional lighting?

• Hot market: every show, every corporate event, every luxury wedding needs professional lighting.

• High pay: a senior lighting technician earns from R$ 800 to R$ 4,000 per event.

• Lighting Designer: from R$ 2,000 to R$ 25,000 per project, depending on the scale.

• Progressive career: rigger → technician → operator → programmer → LD. Each step pays better.

• Versatility: the same knowledge serves shows, corporate events, weddings, theater, TV and architecture.

👤  ROLES, SKILLS AND PAY


The event lighting field has a clear technical hierarchy. Each role has specific skills, defined responsibilities and corresponding pay. Get to know each of them:


1. LIGHTING RIGGER (RIG CREW)


The rigger is the foundation of the operation. Without them, no fixture is in the place the Lighting Designer designed. It's a physical, technical role that demands extreme attention to safety — because everything the rigger puts up in the air can fall.


What they do

Assemble and disassemble support structures (trusses, bars, towers), fix and cable the fixtures, connect DMX and power cables, position the fixtures according to the light plot.

Skills

Reading a light plot, identifying all types of fixture, correct connection of DMX and power cables (Socapex, Powercon, Edison), use of hoists and lifting motors, NR-35 mandatory.

Tools

Combination wrench 13mm and 17mm (for clamps), needle-nose pliers, gaffer tape, headlamps, safety cable, fall-arrest harness and lanyard (work at height).

Not responsible for

Console programming, cue creation, positioning decisions (that's the LD's job). The rigger executes what's in the plot.

Pay

R$ 200 to R$ 600 per event day rate. Large-scale shows with overtime can reach R$ 800-1,000 per day.

Progression

Rigger → Lighting Technician → Console Operator → Programmer → Lighting Designer.



2. LIGHTING TECHNICIAN (LIGHTING TECHNICIAN)


The technician is the evolved rigger. Beyond rigging, they test, diagnose faults, perform basic maintenance on the fixtures and ensure that everything rigged is working before the Lighting Designer arrives at the console.


What they do

Everything the rigger does + tests each fixture after rigging, identifies and resolves faults (lamp out, jammed motor, DMX error), sets the initial focus of the fixtures, supports the programmer during the patch.

Skills

Diagnosing DMX faults (addressing, cable short), identifying fixture failure modes (manual mode, standard mode), replacing lamps in the field, reading the DMX address on the fixture display.

DMX knowledge

Knows that each fixture has a DMX address from 1 to 512, that a DMX universe has 512 channels, that fixtures with many channels (moving heads with 35+ channels) require addressing planning so they don't conflict.

Extra tools

Multimeter for continuity testing of DMX cables, DMX test app on the phone (ArtNetView, MagicQ), XLR/RJ45 cable tester, computer with fixture driver for firmware updates.

Pay

R$ 400 to R$ 1,200 per event. On long tours with complex equipment, it can be a fixed monthly rate of R$ 6,000 to R$ 12,000.

Progression

With mastery of a console (GrandMA, Hog, Chamsys), the technician evolves into an operator. With mastery of design, evolves into an LD.


3. CONSOLE OPERATOR (CONSOLE OPERATOR / BOARD OP)


The console operator runs the show live. They may be executing a program already made by the programmer — pressing Go on each cue — or operating manually, creating the light in real time following the artist. It's a role that demands musicality, reflexes and technical mastery of the console.


What they do

Operates the lighting console during the show — executes pre-programmed cues, makes manual overrides when necessary, follows the show rundown minute by minute, communicates with the producer via radio.

Skills

Mastery of at least one professional console (GrandMA2, GrandMA3, Hog4, Chamsys MQ500). Knowledge of groups, palettes, playbacks and automation macros.

Musicality

The good operator feels the music. They don't just press Go at the right time — they feel when the drop is coming, when the scene should open or close, when the strobe should come in. This can't be programmed: it's felt.

Operator vs programmer difference

The operator executes. The programmer creates. In big shows, they are different people. In smaller events, the same person does both.

Pay

R$ 600 to R$ 2,500 per event. Console operator on a large-scale artist's tour: R$ 8,000 to R$ 18,000/month.

Console

GrandMA is the market standard in Brazil. Knowing GrandMA2/3 is the most valued edge in today's market.


4. LIGHTING PROGRAMMER (PROGRAMMER)


The programmer is the invisible architect of the light show. They receive the project from the Lighting Designer and turn it into scenes, cues, palettes and macros on the console — before the show happens. In big productions, the programmer may work for days or weeks pre-programming every second of the show.


What they do

Creates the complete show programming on the console: patches the fixtures, creates groups, creates color/position/gobo palettes, programs each cue of the rundown, configures timings, creates automation macros and effects.

Skills

Deep mastery of at least one console (GrandMA is mandatory in the touring market). Knowledge of pixel mapping, visualizers (MA 3D, Capture, WYSIWYG), Art-Net and sACN protocols.

3D visualizer

Before the event, the programmer uses 3D visualization software (Capture, WYSIWYG, MA3D) to build a virtual copy of the rig and pre-program everything without needing to be on site. Saves hours of work calls.

Pixel Mapping

Technique of sending video content (images, animations) to LED fixtures capable of displaying pixel by pixel. Creates video effects directly in the lighting, without the need for additional LED screens.

Pay

R$ 1,500 to R$ 6,000 per programming project. International touring programmer: US$ 1,500 to US$ 4,000/week.

Portfolio

The programmer is evaluated by videos of their live work. Quality-recorded shows are the real résumé of the field.


5. LIGHTING DESIGNER (LD)


The Lighting Designer is the top of the creative and technical lighting chain. They create the visual concept of the show — how many and which fixtures, where each one goes, what the aesthetic language of the project is — and answer for the final visual result to the artist and the production company.


What they do

Creates the visual concept of the show, draws the light plot (fixture map), specifies all the equipment, supervises the rigging, guides the programmer, operates or supervises the console during the show.

The light plot

Scale technical document that shows the exact position of each fixture in the rig — top and side view. Produced in AutoCAD, Vectorworks (Spotlight) or lighting-specific software.

Technical rider

The artist's LD produces the lighting rider: list of mandatory fixtures, positions, quantities, accepted consoles and minimum configurations. The local producer has a contractual obligation to meet it.

Skills

Fundamentals of optics and photometry, color theory (RGB, CMY, color temperatures), console mastery, reading and creating light plots, knowledge of all types of fixture on the market, artistic vision.

Communication

The LD needs to communicate with the artist (about the artistic vision), with the producer (about budget and logistics) and with the technical team (about execution). Three different languages in the same project.

Pay

R$ 2,000 to R$ 25,000 per project. International touring LD: US$ 2,500 to US$ 10,000/week + expenses. It's one of the best-paid roles in the events sector.


🔆  LIGHTING EQUIPMENT — EACH FIXTURE AND ITS FUNCTION


Each type of fixture has unique characteristics that make it suitable for specific functions in lighting design. Knowing each one — what it does, how much it consumes, what the angle is, what the power is — is a fundamental skill for any professional in the field.


MOVING FIXTURES — MOVING HEADS


Moving heads are the most versatile fixtures and the most present in professional shows. They are motorized fixtures that move on two axes (pan and tilt), allowing dynamic effects to be created in real time via DMX.


FIXTURE

POWER / TYPE

MAIN FUNCTION

USE EXAMPLE

Moving Head Beam

Fixed focus 0°-4°. 200W to 700W LED or MSR. Rotating gobo, prism, frost.

Beam: a sharp, crisp beam of light that cuts through the air. Creates ray effects, formations, aerial grids.

Clay Paky Sharpy, Robe BMFL Blade. Dramatic artist entrances, electronic music drops.

Moving Head Spot

Variable focus 5°-25°. 300W to 1,200W. Interchangeable gobos, iris, CMY.

Projects shapes and textures on the stage or on the artist. More versatile than the beam — does spot and wash.

Martin MAC Viper, Robe Pointe. Artist follow spot, gobo projection on the backdrop.

Moving Head Wash

Wide focus 10°-60°. 200W to 800W LED RGBW. Motorized zoom.

Color wash over large areas. Creates atmosphere, fills the background, tints the entire stage.

Robe ROBIN 600E Wash, Martin MAC Aura. Color wash on the audience, color backdrop, key light.

Moving Head Hybrid

Combines beam + spot + wash. Zoom from 0° to 40°+. 400W to 1,000W.

The most versatile fixture on the market — does everything. High cost, high creative capacity.

Robe BMFL Followspot, GLP X4 Bar. High-level production where few fixtures need to do a lot.

Moving Head Strip (Bar)

Bar of RGBW LEDs with movement. From 4 to 20 individual LEDs on a strip. Individual zoom per LED.

Pixel effects, color waves along the bar, dynamic footlight, cyclorama lighting.

GLP Impression X4 Bar. Stage footlight, wave effects on truss arcs.


STATIC FIXTURES — PAR, FRESNEL, PROFILE AND LED WASH


FIXTURE

POWER / TYPE

MAIN FUNCTION

USE EXAMPLE

PAR LED (LED PAR64)

54 to 216 RGBWA+UV LEDs. 100W to 300W. Fixed angle: 15° to 60°.

Color wash over specific areas. Cyclorama lighting, backdrop, truss rings.

PAR64 LED RGBW 18x18W. Color on the back of the stage, lighting musicians in fixed positions.

Conventional PAR (HiPAR)

PAR64 lamp of 500W or 1,000W halogen (GX16d). Fixed angle according to lamp (VNSP to WFL).

Powerful, directional, low-cost light. Widely used in low-budget shows and theaters.

PAR64 CP62 (medium). Side fill light on the stage, lighting the drum area.

Fresnel

Fresnel lens for soft, diffuse light. 300W to 2,000W. Manual zoom by focusing the fixture.

Soft, homogeneous light for artist key light, fill light and soft light on the stage.

Fresnel 1,000W. Key light on the lead vocalist, stage fill in smaller shows.

Profile (Ellipsoidal / Leko)

Condenser lens with shutters to cut the light into precise shapes. 300W to 1,200W LED.

Precise projection of light onto defined areas without spill. Full control of the beam shape.

ETC Source Four LED, Robert Juliat. Key light on a solo artist, gobo projection on a wall.

LED Strip / Batten

Strip of RGBW LEDs from 0.5m to 3m. Individually controllable pixels. 50W to 300W.

Stage footlight, truss edge, cyclorama, underglow effect on structures.

Showtec Pixel Bar. Colored stage footlight, bottom edge of an LED wall, color cyclorama.

Blinder / Audience Light

High-power fixture aimed at the audience. 4 to 8 lamps of 650W or high-power LEDs.

Momentarily blinds the audience to create a dramatic effect or reveals the artist out of blackout.

4-eye LED Blinder. Blackout-reveal effect at a show opening, climax drops.


CONTROL — CONSOLES, DMX AND PROTOCOLS


The lighting console is the instrument of the operator and the programmer. It's through it that each fixture receives DMX instructions — the digital 512-channel protocol that commands position, color, intensity, gobo and all the parameters of each light in the rig.


EQUIPMENT

TECHNICAL SPECIFICATION

FUNCTION IN EVENTS

GrandMA3 Full-Size

2,048 physical parameters (expandable to 250,000 over the network). 30 motorized faders, 5 touch screens. Supports Art-Net and sACN. Weight: 46 kg.

Main console for large-scale shows and international tours. Absolute market standard. Artist riders require GrandMA in 80% of cases.

GrandMA3 Light

Compact version of the MA3. 8,192 parameters. 15 faders. 2 touch screens. Weight: 18 kg.

Medium-scale shows, use as a backup console in large productions, programming while traveling.

GrandMA3 onPC

GrandMA3 software running on a Windows computer. 256 free parameters, expandable with MA-Net hardware.

Learning, pre-programming in the office, operating small events with an MA Node for DMX output.

Chamsys MQ500

4,096 parameters. 15 direct DMX universes. 30 faders. Lower cost than GrandMA. Supports Art-Net and sACN.

Professional console at an affordable cost. Widely used in medium-scale national shows, theater and corporate events.

ETC Eos Ti

Focused on theater and architectural lighting. 250,000 parameters. Excellent for shows with many theater cues.

Theater, opera, television, corporate events with many pre-programmed scenes and little movement.

DMX Node / Node (MA Node, Art-Net Node)

Converts network signal (Art-Net / sACN via Ethernet cable) into DMX512 signal (XLR5 or RJ45 cable). 1 to 8 universes per node.

Distributes the DMX signal throughout the event without cable-distance limitation. Each node feeds up to 512 fixtures in the corresponding universe.

DMX Cable (XLR-5 / XLR-3)

Cable of 120 Ω impedance. Two pairs of conductors + shielding. 5-pin XLR connector (standard) or 3-pin (adapted).

Transports the DMX signal from the console (or node) to the fixtures. Maximum 300m per segment without a repeater.

DMX Distributor (Splitter)

Amplifies and distributes the DMX signal from one input to 2 to 8 independent outputs. Prevents signal reflection in large rigs.

Connects the console to multiple fixture branches without signal degradation. Mandatory in rigs with more than 30 fixtures.


ESSENTIAL LIGHTING ACCESSORIES


ACCESSORY

WHAT IT IS

WHAT IT'S FOR

Clamp (Half Coupler)

Cast aluminum clamp of 50mm or 38mm. Comes with an M12 safety bolt.

Fixes the fixture to the truss. The clamp must be the right size for the truss — a 50mm clamp on a 50mm truss. A loose clamp is a guaranteed fall.

Safety Cable (Safety Cable)

3mm stainless steel cable with a crimped loop at both ends. Lengths from 30cm to 1.5m.

Mandatory safety on all suspended fixtures. If the clamp comes loose, the safety holds the fixture. Without a safety cable, the fixture can kill whoever is below.

Gobo (Pattern / Effect)

Steel or glass disc with a cut-out pattern. Diameters: A-size (100mm), B-size (86mm), M-size (65.5mm), E-size (37.5mm).

Inserted into the gobo slot of spots and moving heads to project textures and shapes. Stars, trees, flames, abstracts, text, logos — endless possibilities.

Gel (Color Filter)

Colored polycarbonate plastic film in hundreds of standardized colors (Lee Filters, Rosco). Size: 50x60cm sheets.

Puts color on the output of conventional fixtures (PAR, Fresnel, Profile). Identified by its numbering: Lee 182 (Light Red), Lee 201 (Full Blue CTO).

Barn Door (Barn Door)

Four adjustable metal flaps in front of the fixture.

Controls the shape of the light beam on conventional fixtures. Closes the flaps to limit where the light falls.

Haze Machine (Hazer)

Machine that produces a fine-particle haze from a water-based or mineral-oil-based fluid.

Makes the light beams visible in the air — the most dramatic element of any show. Without haze, moving head beams are invisible in the dark.

Fog Machine (Fog)

Produces a denser, short-lasting fog. Water-based or glycol-based fluid.

Dramatic apparition effects out of blackout, covering the stage for a reveal, creating a suspenseful atmosphere.

Dimmer Rack

Power-control module that attenuates the electrical energy supplied to conventional fixtures via DMX.

Controls the intensity of fixtures with halogen or incandescent lamps. Modern LED fixtures have an internal dimmer.

🎪  LIGHTING BY EVENT TYPE — WHAT CHANGES IN EACH SCENARIO


Each type of event has a different lighting language. What works at an electronic rock show doesn't work at a luxury wedding. The LD who understands this difference — and knows how to adapt their approach — is the professional with a full calendar all year round.


LIVE MUSIC SHOW — ROCK, POP, ELECTRONIC


Goal of the light

Create visual impact, amplify the emotion of the music, make the audience feel the show with their body, not just their ears.

Typical fixtures

Moving head beams (Sharpy, Pointe), moving head wash (MAC Aura), LED PARs, strobes, blinders, LED bar for footlight, hazer.

Operation style

Dynamic and reactive to the music. The operator follows the drops, the fills, the silences. Every beat has its corresponding light.

Dominant colors

Rock: blue, cool white, intense red. Pop: vibrant colors, soft pastels in the ballad, chromatic explosion in the chorus. Electronic: cyan, magenta, UV, white beams, intense strobe.

Common mistakes

Constant white light without movement (looks like a rehearsal). Lack of haze (invisible beams). Strobe at a random rhythm unrelated to the music.

LD pay

R$ 3,000 to R$ 15,000 per show, depending on scale. Big artists' shows: R$ 10,000 to R$ 30,000+ per night.


CORPORATE EVENT — CONFERENCE, LAUNCH, AWARDS


Goal of the light

Create a professional environment, highlight the speaker, ensure cameras capture sharp images, reinforce the brand's visual identity.

Typical fixtures

Fresnel or Profile LED for the speaker's key light, soft LED wash for fill, a discreet moving head spot to highlight a product, architectural lighting of the space, a gobo with the company logo.

Color temperature

4,000K to 5,600K for good camera capture. Avoid mixing color temperatures — the camera can't balance white with 3,200K and 5,600K simultaneously on the same stage.

Logo projection

Glass gobo engraved with the company logo projected on the floor or the backdrop by the ellipsoidal/profile. An elegant, high-impact effect for launches.

Common mistakes

Light that's too hard on the speaker's face (nose shadow). Lack of side fill (half the face in the dark). A light color that distorts the color of the clothing or the product.

LD pay

R$ 2,000 to R$ 8,000 per corporate event. Launch events for big brands reach R$ 15,000+.


LUXURY WEDDING — CEREMONY AND PARTY


Goal of the light

Create romance, highlight the bride at the moment of her entrance, transform the existing décor into a cinematic setting, ensure beautiful photos.

Typical fixtures

Soft moving head spot as a follow spot for the bride (with a frost diffuser), amber/lilac wall uplights to create atmosphere, low-intensity LED PAR, leaf gobo on the floor/ceiling, candlelight on the table.

Uplighting (base light)

Fixtures aimed at the walls and columns of the hall from bottom to top. They create depth and volume in the space. The color changes with the moment: ceremony (warm white), cocktail (amber), party (dynamic color).

Leaf / star gobo

Projecting a foliage or star pattern on the dance floor or the walls of the hall. Creates a magical environment with no additional décor cost.

First kiss moment

The LD prepares a specific cue: all the moving heads point at the couple, the walls go soft pink, the rest of the hall goes to 20% intensity. The couple becomes the absolute protagonist of the space.

LD pay

R$ 1,500 to R$ 8,000 per wedding, depending on scale. Weddings in luxury venues in São Paulo, Rio and destinations like Gramado reach R$ 15,000+.


OPEN-AIR FESTIVAL — LARGE-SCALE DESIGN


Goal of the light

Create an imposing visual identity, differentiate each stage, create effects visible from 200 meters away, surprise an audience that has already seen a lot of light.

Typical fixtures

Large high-power moving head beams (Robe BMFL, Martin MAC III), RGB laser of 20W+, followspots of 2,500W (Robert Juliat, Strong), large-area LED wall, long-distance hazer.

Laser in shows

The laser is the only lighting element that creates perfectly sharp lines in the air. It requires an ANATEL license in Brazil, a certified operator and a 3m exclusion zone above the audience. Incomparable visual impact.

Large-scale LED wall

High-resolution LED panel (P3.9 to P10) as the stage backdrop. Replaces the traditional projection screen with 50x greater brightness. Works with quality even in broad daylight.

Lighting budget

At big festivals (Lollapalooza, Rock in Rio, Primavera Sound), the lighting budget alone can reach R$ 500,000 to R$ 2,000,000 per stage per edition.

LD pay

Large-scale festival LD: US$ 5,000 to US$ 20,000 per edition + travel + lodging. It's the top of the career in Brazil.

📋  THE WORK PROCESS — FROM BRIEFING TO DEBRIEFING


A professional lighting project begins long before the rigging and ends long after the show. Knowing each stage of the process is what sets a professional apart from an occasional operator.


STAGE 1 — BRIEFING AND CONCEPT


Meeting with the artist / producer

Understand: what's the energy of the show? What's the visual concept? Are there references from previous shows? What are the most important moments of the setlist?

Setlist analysis

Each song has a different emotional temperature. The LD maps the emotional arc of the show and designs the light palette for each moment.

Site analysis (venue walk)

Visit to the site to measure dimensions, check available rigging points, electrical capacity, ceiling height, wall color.

Rig definition

Based on the briefing and the venue, the LD defines which fixtures to use, how many of each type, where each one will go.

STAGE 2 — LIGHT PLOT DESIGN


Software

Vectorworks Spotlight (market standard), AutoCAD, or for small events: Capture with a simplified plot function.

What the light plot contains

Exact position of each fixture (with a numerical identification), electrical circuit of each one, DMX address, fixture type, clamp type, position of each element of the structure (truss, bar, tower).

Plot deliverables

Top view plot, front view plot, equipment list, circuit list. These documents guide the entire rigging team.

Communicating the plot

The light plot is sent to the local production company in advance — usually 1 to 2 weeks ahead. The local production company uses the plot to rig everything before the LD arrives.

STAGE 3 — WORK CALL (RIGGING)


What the work call is

The work session for rigging the rig. It starts with delivering the plot to the rig crew, who rig the fixtures as specified.

Rigging order

1. Assemble and secure the trusses and structures. 2. Hoist the trusses. 3. Fix the fixtures with clamps and safety cables. 4. Connect the power cables (Socapex or individual). 5. Connect the DMX cables. 6. Power up and test each fixture individually.

Rig test (rig check)

After rigging, the LD or technician does the rig check: each fixture is tested individually — it powers up, lights, its DMX address is verified, it does full pan and tilt, its color is checked. Any fault is fixed before the patch on the console.

Patch on the console

The LD or programmer configures the patch on the console: associates the DMX address of each fixture with its fixture number on the console. After the patch, each fixture responds when called on the console.

STAGE 4 — PROGRAMMING AND REHEARSAL


Palette programming

Creates the color palettes, position palettes and gobo (beam) palettes that will be used during the show. The palettes speed up live operation.

Cue programming

Each cue is a memorized light state. A medium-scale show has from 50 to 500 cues. A large-scale show can have 1,000+ cues with automatic timings synchronized to the music.

Rehearsal with the artist

The most important moment before the show. The LD operates the show while the artist rehearses — adjusting intensities, follow-spot positions and colors according to the artist's feedback.

Follow spot adjustment

The followspot operators (when there are any) receive the rundown of who to follow and when. Each followspot has a filter color and an intensity defined by the LD.


⚠️  COMMON MISTAKES AND BEST PRACTICES IN LIGHTING


❌  MISTAKE / AMATEUR

✅  CORRECT / PROFESSIONAL

Turning on the follow spot without defining the filter color — raw white light on the artist

Always define the follow spot color before the show. 1/4 CTO gel (Lee 206) is the standard for skin.

Placing fixtures on the grid without a safety cable — 'the clamp is nice and tight'

Safety cable mandatory on every suspended fixture. A clamp can loosen with the show's vibration.

Using duplicate DMX addresses in the same universe — two fixtures responding together

Plan the patch before rigging. Verify the address on each fixture before powering up the console.

Leaving the hazer off and wondering why the beams don't show

Turn on the hazer at least 20 minutes before the show to saturate the air with haze. Immediate result.

Programming the show with just one color palette — everything the same color the whole time

Create varied color palettes and progress throughout the setlist. Light tells the story of the music.

Not backing up the showfile — losing the programming to a hardware failure

Save the showfile on the console AND on an external USB every hour of programming. Backup is a rule, not an option.

Connecting the DMX cable directly without a distributor when there are more than 30 fixtures

Use a DMX splitter for branches with more than 30 fixtures. Prevents signal reflection and instability.

Operating the show without communication with the stage manager — light out of cue

Open radio with the stage manager the whole time. The light needs to happen at the right moment.

Using the same color and intensity for the entire show — static, without emotion

Light is dynamic. It changes with the music, with the moment of the show, with the artist's energy.

Ignoring the artist's technical rider and using other fixtures — show cancellation

The technical rider is a contract. Fixtures not accepted by the artist's LD lead to cancellation or a rider fee.

🎯  Conclusion — Lighting is the emotion the audience doesn't know it's feeling

Professional lighting in events is one of the most creative and technically demanding fields in the sector. The professional who masters this field — from the rigger who puts the fixture in the right place to the Lighting Designer who creates the emotion of the show — is indispensable in any quality production.


The market is growing, technology is advancing and events are getting bigger. The professional trained by SMU Produções who enters this field with solid technical knowledge, a developed artistic vision and impeccable professional conduct will find a market that needs exactly what they have to offer.


🏅  The path to becoming a lighting professional

LEVEL 1 — Rigger: learn to rig fixtures, read a light plot, use a safety cable. (0 to 6 months)

LEVEL 2 — Technician: master DMX diagnosis, addressing, basic patch. (6 months to 2 years)

LEVEL 3 — Operator: master a console (GrandMA onPC first). Operate small events. (1 to 3 years)

LEVEL 4 — Programmer: pre-program shows in a 3D visualizer, create complete showfiles. (2 to 5 years)

LEVEL 5 — Lighting Designer: create concepts, draw light plots, sign off on projects. (5+ years)


Don't skip steps — each level builds the next. The LD who can't rig loses the team's respect.


"The audience doesn't see the light.

The audience feels what the light does to them."

SMU PRODUÇÕES

Professional School of Events

Professional Video at Events

LED Screens, Cameras, Switchers, IMAG and Streaming


"The audience doesn't see the stage.

The audience sees what you decide to show — and how you decide to show it."

📋  What Professional Video at Events Is

The video professional at events is responsible for everything the audience SEES on screens. At large-scale shows, an audience of 50,000 people cannot see the artist's face clearly — it is the video system that brings that image to each person at the event. At corporate events, video conveys the brand's credibility. At conferences, it distributes the content to the remote audience.


Video at events is a technical and creative field simultaneously. It requires mastery of broadcast equipment, the ability to make decisions in real time, knowledge of digital and analog signals, and a trained eye for visual composition. The professional who masters this field is in one of the hottest and least saturated areas of the Brazilian events market.


📺  Why learn professional video for events?

• Booming market: live streaming, hybrid events and IMAG have tripled the demand for video technicians since 2020.

• High pay: an LED technician earns from R$ 500 to R$ 2,000/event. Video director: R$ 3,000 to R$ 20,000/project.

• Equipment values those who know how to operate it: Blackmagic ATEM, Resolume, Disguise — whoever masters these systems never runs out of work.

• Total versatility: the same knowledge works for shows, TV, corporate events, weddings and sports broadcasts.

👤  ROLES, SKILLS AND PAY


The video field at events has a clear technical hierarchy, with well-defined roles and specific pay for each level. Knowing this structure is the first step to planning your career in this field.


1. LED / PROJECTION TECHNICIAN


This is the entry-level role in the events video field. The LED technician assembles, aligns, calibrates and operates the LED panels and projectors. It is physical and technical work — LED panels weigh between 5 and 15 kg each, have sharp edges and require precision in fitting and calibration.


What they do

Assembles the LED cabinets, connects the data and power cables, configures the video processor, calibrates brightness and color, operates the system during the event.

Skills

Knowledge of video processors (NovaStar, Brompton, ColorLight), physical fitting of cabinets, data cabling (Cat6/fiber), resolution and pixel pitch.

Tools

Allen key, power screwdriver, multimeter, laptop with processor software, color alignment app, Cat6 cables and optical fiber.

Physical risk

LED cabinets have sharp edges — impact-resistant gloves are mandatory. Heavy panels require correct lifting technique (NR-17). High-current electrical work — NR-10 applies.

Pay

R$ 400 to R$ 1,500 per event. On international productions with a large-scale LED wall: R$ 1,500 to R$ 3,000/day.

Progression

LED Technician → Processor Operator → Switcher Technician → Switcher Operator → Video Director.

2. SWITCHER OPERATOR / CUTTING DESK


The switcher operator is the one who makes the live cuts. It is the most visible role and the one with the greatest technical responsibility during the event — every cut the audience sees on screen went through your decision, your hand and your timing. It is a role that requires both technical mastery and editorial instinct.


What they do

Operates the cutting desk during the event: switches between cameras, inserts graphics, manages transitions, ensures the correct content appears on each screen at the right moment.

Skills

Mastery of at least one professional switcher (Blackmagic ATEM, Roland V-8HD, Analog Way Midra). Knowledge of signal routing, video formats (1080p, 4K, SDI, HDMI), cut timing.

Editorial instinct

The good switcher operator senses when the scene is about to change. They are already on the right camera before the moment happens — not after. This develops with practice and the study of audiovisual directing.

Operator/director difference

The operator executes the cuts technically. The director decides which cuts to make. At small events, they are the same person. In large productions, they are separate roles with constant communication via intercom.

Pay

R$ 600 to R$ 2,500 per event. Experienced operator at a large-scale show: R$ 2,000 to R$ 5,000/event. Artist tour: R$ 8,000 to R$ 15,000/month.

Main console

Blackmagic ATEM is the market's entry standard. Roland V-Series for corporate events. Analog Way and Christie for high-budget productions.

3. CAMERAMAN / CAMERA OPERATOR


The cameraman captures the images that the switcher will cut. At live events, unlike film or TV, there is no second take. Each camera position has a specific role in the IMAG system, and the cameraman needs to know what their function is within the whole in order to anticipate what the director will need.


What they do

Operates fixed or mobile cameras during the event: frames, focuses, follows the artist, keeps correct exposure, responds to the director's instructions via intercom/IFB.

Typical positions

Fixed front camera (wide shot of the stage), pit camera (close-up of the performer, low angle), shoulder camera (mobile, follows the artist on stage), stage POV camera (artist's angle toward the audience).

Equipment

Sony PXW-Z280, Sony FX6, Blackmagic Studio Camera, Canon C300 cameras. Lenses: 70-200mm (pit camera), 24-70mm (stage camera), wide angle (wide camera).

Intercom/IFB

The cameraman receives instructions from the director through the IFB (Interruptible FoldBack) — an earpiece with a feed from the director. 'Camera 2, close on the face', 'Camera 3, follow the drummer', 'Standby camera 1'.

Pay

R$ 400 to R$ 1,500 per event. Pit cameraman at a large-scale show: R$ 1,000 to R$ 2,500/event. On a live TV broadcast: R$ 1,500 to R$ 4,000/day.

Differentiator

A cameraman who understands music anticipates the right moments — closes in on the face before the vocal, opens up at the chorus. Musical timing is as important as camera technique.

4. VJ — VIDEO JOCKEY


The VJ creates and operates visual content in real time. Unlike the technician who plays back pre-recorded content, the VJ manipulates, combines and synchronizes visuals with live music — creating an audiovisual experience that responds to the energy of the moment. It is the most creative and expressive role in the video field.


What they do

Creates and manipulates visual content in real time: motion graphics, video loops, generative art, surface mapping (projection mapping). Synchronizes everything with the live music.

Software

Resolume Avenue/Arena (market standard for VJs), Notch (real-time generative), TouchDesigner (advanced generative programming), Disguise (media server for large productions).

Content

Organizes a clip library by BPM. Creates layers of visuals that combine in real time. Maps color, transition and composition effects to a MIDI keyboard or controller.

Pixel Mapping

A technique of sending video content to the lighting's LED fixtures — creating a pixel-by-pixel image on the rig's lights. Requires integration with the lighting console (GrandMA via Art-Net).

Pay

R$ 500 to R$ 3,000 per event. VJ at an electronic music festival: R$ 1,500 to R$ 8,000/night. Creative VJ with a strong portfolio: international projects of US$ 2,000 to US$ 10,000.

Portfolio

A VJ is judged by videos of their live work. Publishing sets on Instagram and Vimeo with good image quality and synchronized audio is the real résumé of the field.

5. VIDEO DIRECTOR


The Video Director is the creative and technical top of the field. They do not operate any equipment during the show — they coordinate the entire video team, make the editorial decisions and ensure the final visual result is coherent with the event's concept and the expectations of the artist or client.


What they do

Plans the camera system, defines placements, creates the event's visual script, coordinates cameramen via intercom, instructs the switcher operator on the cuts, supervises the VJ and the LED technician.

Camera plan

A document that defines: how many cameras, where each one goes, which lens it uses, what each camera's function is in the system (wide, close, mobility), and which camera is the priority at each moment of the show.

Communication

The director speaks to everyone via intercom simultaneously: 'Camera 2 standby, camera 1 on screen, camera 3 follow the guitar solo'. Clarity of communication is the most important skill in this role.

Video rider

The artist's video LD produces the technical rider: minimum cameras, LED screen resolution, accepted formats, mandatory camera position. The local director is obligated to comply.

Pay

R$ 2,000 to R$ 20,000 per project. Video director on an international tour: US$ 2,500 to US$ 8,000/week + expenses. It is the highest pay in the video field.

Progression

Technician → Switcher Operator → Experienced Cameraman → Assistant Director → Video Director. Each step requires having operated the previous one with excellence.


6. STREAMING TECHNICIAN


The streaming technician is the professional who connects the event to the remote world. At hybrid events and live broadcasts, they ensure the video and audio signal reaches all platforms with quality — YouTube, Instagram Live, Zoom, corporate platforms — without interruption and without loss of quality.


What they do

Configures the streaming encoder, sets bitrate and resolution, monitors the health of the stream in real time, manages latency, ensures internet connection redundancy.

Software

OBS Studio (market standard), vMix, Wirecast, Livestream Studio. For corporate: dedicated platforms (StreamYard, Restream, Vimeo Live).

Hardware encoder

Teradek Bolt, AJA HELO, Cerevo LiveShell — dedicated encoders that process the camera/switcher signal directly to streaming without relying on a computer.

Internet connection

Never rely on Wi-Fi for professional broadcasting. Use a dedicated Ethernet cable + a 4G/5G modem as backup. Bonding of multiple connections (LiveU, Haivision) for critical events.

Pay

R$ 400 to R$ 2,000 per event. Streaming technician for international corporate events: R$ 1,500 to R$ 5,000/event. Accelerated growth since 2020.

Differentiator

Whoever masters streaming AND switcher operation is the most complete professional in today's market. Most know one or the other — whoever knows both has a full schedule.

📺  VIDEO EQUIPMENT — EACH ONE AND ITS FUNCTION


Each piece of equipment in the video chain has a specific function. Understanding what each one does — and how they connect — is essential to building a coherent system and diagnosing failures during the event.


LED SCREENS — MODULAR PANELS


LED screens are the main video display at professional events. Unlike projectors, they work with any level of ambient light — they can be used in daylight at maximum quality. They are assembled from individual cabinets fitted onto a metal structure.

📐  How to calculate the correct pixel pitch for your event

Rule of thumb: minimum viewing distance (in meters) = pixel pitch (in mm) × 3


Example: P3.9 panel → minimum distance = 3.9 × 3 = 11.7 meters

If the closest audience is 8 meters away, use P2.9 (minimum distance = 8.7m) or P2.6.


Golden rule: if you err toward a smaller pitch (more pixels), the image looks better.

If you err toward a larger pitch (fewer pixels), the image pixelates — a visible and embarrassing mistake.


VIDEO PROCESSORS FOR LED — THE BRAIN OF THE SYSTEM


The video processor (also called the LED controller or video processor) is the equipment that receives the video signal from the computer or switcher and distributes it to the LED panels. It handles the conversion, scaling, color calibration and physical mapping of the panels on the screen.

VIDEO SIGNALS — SDI, HDMI, NDI AND FIBER


Understanding video signal protocols is essential to building failure-free systems. Choosing the wrong cable or connector type causes signal loss, image degradation or the inability to connect equipment.


SIGNAL / PROTOCOL

TECHNICAL SPECIFICATION

USE AT EVENTS

SDI (Serial Digital Interface)

BNC coaxial, 75 Ω. 3G-SDI: 1080p60. 12G-SDI: 4K60. Distance: up to 100m without a repeater. No handshake — instant image.

Professional broadcast standard. Camera → switcher → monitor. The only reliable protocol for long distances without conversion.

HDMI 2.0 / 2.1

Cable with a type-A connector. HDMI 2.0: 4K60. HDMI 2.1: 8K60. Maximum distance: 7.5m passive, 30m active. HDCP handshake can cause problems.

Connecting computers, laptops, projectors and monitors over short distances. At events, always prefer SDI for long distances.

DisplayPort / Thunderbolt

DP 1.4: 8K30. USB-C with Thunderbolt 4: 40 Gbps. Short distance only.

Computer output to an LED processor or streaming encoder. Short cable, less than 2m in most cases.

NDI (Network Device Interface)

IP over Ethernet protocol. Latency: 1 frame. Supports hundreds of streams on a Gigabit network.

Virtual cameras, network sources between computers, switcher integration with automation systems. Growing in hybrid corporate events.

Optical Fiber (SFP)

Single-mode: up to 10km. Multi-mode: up to 550m. Total immunity to electromagnetic interference.

Long distances at shows: helicopter camera, arena-top camera, signal between stage and FOH beyond 100m. Uses an SFP transceiver at both ends.

SMPTE 311M (Camera fiber)

Optical fiber + copper in a single cable. Carries video, power, intercom and control to the camera in a single cable.

Broadcast cameras connected to the CCU (Camera Control Unit) in live TV productions at events. The sports-coverage standard.

🎬  IMAG — IMAGE MAGNIFICATION: THE HEART OF LIVE VIDEO


IMAG stands for Image Magnification — magnifying the stage image onto side or central screens. It is the system that allows an audience of 40,000 people in a stadium to see the artist's face as clearly as someone in the third row. It is considered the noblest use of live video at events.


HOW THE IMAG SYSTEM WORKS


Capture

Strategically positioned cameras capture different angles of the artist: front close-up (pit camera), profile (side camera), wide angle (FOH wide camera), stage cameras (POV).

Routing

The SDI signal of each camera goes to the switcher via SDI cable or fiber. The switcher receives all the simultaneous feeds and the operator selects which one goes on air.

Live cut

The switcher operator makes the cuts in real time following the director's instruction via intercom. Each cut needs to be musical — on the beat, on the turnaround, at the right dramatic moment.

Distribution

The program signal (switcher output) goes to the LED processors of the IMAG screens. Each screen displays the image in real time, with minimal latency (less than 1 frame = less than 16ms).

Critical latency

In IMAG, any latency above 2 frames (33ms) creates visual desynchronization between what the audience sees live and what appears on screen — the artist's movement arrives before the image. This is unacceptable.

IMAG screens

Usually placed at the sides of the stage, at an elevated height. Minimum size for 10,000 people: 6m × 4m. For stadiums: 12m × 8m or larger. Pixel pitch: P4.8 to P6.0 for the typical distance of 30-80m.

📡  LIVE STREAMING — CONNECTING THE EVENT TO THE WORLD


Streaming has transformed local events into global experiences. A show in São Paulo can be watched live by 500,000 people on YouTube as it happens. A corporate event in Brasília can have participants from 40 countries via a digital platform. The streaming technician is the one who makes this possible — and the one who answers when it goes down.


STREAMING SIGNAL CHAIN — FROM STAGE TO PLATFORM


1. Video source

The video switcher (program output) delivers the SDI or HDMI signal to the streaming encoder.

2. Audio source

The mixing console delivers a stereo output (balanced XLR or digital AES/EBU) to the encoder. Reference level: –14 dBFS (LUFS) for streaming platforms.

3. Streaming encoder

Converts the video + audio signals into a compressed stream (H.264 or H.265) with a bitrate appropriate for the platform. Hardware (Teradek, AJA HELO) or software (OBS Studio, vMix).

4. Internet connection

A dedicated Ethernet cable of at least 10 Mbps upload per 1080p60 stream. A 4G/5G modem as automatic backup. Speed test BEFORE the event — upload, not download.

5. CDN / Platform

The stream reaches the platform's CDN (Content Delivery Network) (YouTube, Twitch, Facebook), which distributes it to all viewers. Latency: 10-30 seconds (HLS) or 3-5 seconds (LL-HLS).

6. Monitoring

The technician monitors the platform's dashboard (YouTube Studio, Twitch Dashboard) — stream health, audience peaks, incoming bitrate, dropped frames.


STREAMING SETTINGS — BITRATE AND QUALITY


QUALITY

RESOLUTION / FPS

RECOMMENDED BITRATE

MINIMUM UPLOAD REQUIRED

⚡  Golden rule of professional streaming

Available upload ÷ 2 = maximum safe streaming bitrate.


If the line's upload is 20 Mbps: maximum safe bitrate = 10 Mbps (10,000 kbps).

Using 100% of the available upload guarantees interruptions when someone else uses the network.

Always use dedicated cabling for streaming — NEVER Wi-Fi at a professional event.

Always have a 4G/5G modem as backup, configured and ready before the event begins.

🎪  VIDEO BY EVENT TYPE — WHAT CHANGES IN EACH SCENARIO


Each type of event requires a different approach to video. What works at a rock show does not work at a corporate conference. The professional who understands these differences and adapts the system correctly is the one the market hires back.


MUSIC SHOW — IMAG AND VISUAL IMPACT


Video objective

Amplify the artist's presence, create visual impact synchronized with the music, bring the artist's face to those who are far away.

Typical system

Backdrop LED wall (P3.9 to P6.0), 2 side IMAG screens (P4.8 to P6.0), 3 to 6 cameras (pit, wide, mobile, PTZ), SDI switcher, VJ with Resolume for backdrop content.

Cutting style

Dynamic and musical. Cuts on the beat, anticipation of the emotional moment, close-up on the chorus, wide on the drop. Cutting speed increases with the energy of the music.

AV synchronization

The backdrop video (VJ) needs to be in sync with the music and the lighting. Video, lighting and audio technicians must be in constant communication during the show.

Latency

LED wall: maximum 1-2 frames (16-33ms). IMAG: maximum 2 frames. Any greater latency is perceived by the artist as a 'ghost' on the screen.

Director's pay

R$ 3,000 to R$ 15,000 per show. Shows by big artists: R$ 10,000 to R$ 30,000+.


CORPORATE EVENT — CLARITY AND RELIABILITY


Video objective

Present the speaker clearly, display slides and content without failure, ensure that TV and streaming cameras capture a professional image.

Typical system

1 to 2 projection or LED screens (P2.9 to P3.9), a stage camera for IMAG, a speaker-following camera, a switcher to alternate between slide + camera, a stream for remote participants.

Lower thirds

Speaker identification graphics inserted by the switcher or the streaming software. Name, title, company — appears on screen during the talk, clean and without interfering with the image.

Teleprompter

At high-level corporate events, the speaker reads the speech on a teleprompter positioned on the camera. The video technician operates the text scroll speed in sync with the speech.

Backup

Every corporate system needs a plan B: a backup laptop with the presentation, an extra HDMI cable, a backup switcher. A corporate event that goes down stays in the client's memory forever.

Director's pay

R$ 2,000 to R$ 10,000 per corporate event. Big-brand launch events reach R$ 20,000+.

📋  THE WORK PROCESS — FROM BRIEFING TO DEBRIEFING


A professional video project begins long before the setup and ends with the archiving of the material. Knowing each stage of the process is what sets the events professional apart from the weekend camera operator.


STAGE 1 — BRIEFING AND VIDEO PLAN


Meeting with production

Understand: what is the objective of the video at the event? IMAG only? Streaming? Recording? Backdrop content?

Venue analysis

Stage dimensions, ceiling height, available rigging points for cameras and screens, electrical capacity, distance of the closest and farthest audience.

System definition

The correct pixel pitch for the venue, the number of cameras needed and their positions, a switcher suited to the number of sources, an internet connectivity plan for streaming.

Video rider

Check whether the artist has a video rider: mandatory cameras, accepted formats, minimum screen resolution, a specific required camera position.

STAGE 2 — SETUP AND TESTING


LED setup

Assemble the cabinets in the correct order (bottom to top), connect data and power, turn on the processor, map the panels in the software (physical layout), calibrate brightness and color uniformity.

Camera rack

Connect all cameras to the switcher via SDI or fiber, verify that each camera appears on the multi-view monitor, adjust the base exposure of each camera with the same chip chart.

Switcher test

Check each source on the switcher, test cuts between cameras, configure aux outs for IMAG and streaming separately, set up macros for the most-used transitions.

Streaming test

Run a 30-minute test stream before the event with a real internet load, check the bitrate, dropped frames and the image quality received by the client or by YouTube.

STAGE 3 — SHOW AND POST-EVENT


Camera briefing

30 minutes before: a meeting with all the cameramen to go over the camera plan, confirm positions, test the intercom and IFB of each camera.

During the show

Director via intercom, operator at the switcher, technician monitoring the LED processor and streaming. Any problem is communicated immediately by radio to the producer.

Recording

If the event is recorded for later use: verify that recording is active on the encoder or switcher. Do not rely on streaming alone — always make a local backup.

Teardown

Turn off the screens progressively, pack the LED cabinets in the reverse order of assembly, record any equipment damage before returning it.

Material delivery

If a recording was contracted: export the file, check quality, deliver by the agreed deadline. Event material has a delivery window — there is no second take.

⚠️  COMMON MISTAKES AND BEST PRACTICES IN EVENTS VIDEO


❌  MISTAKE — AMATEUR

✅  CORRECT — PROFESSIONAL

Using Wi-Fi for streaming at a professional event

Dedicated Ethernet cable + 4G backup. Wi-Fi at an event is guaranteed interference.

Assembling the LED wall without mapping the panels in the processor — cut or inverted image

Map the physical layout of the cabinets in the processor software before turning on any content.

Not testing the stream with a real load before the event

Run a 30-minute test stream with the venue's internet before the event begins.

Pit camera without intercom with the director — cutting at the wrong time

IFB on all cameramen before the show. Without communication with the director, IMAG becomes random.

Not having internet backup for streaming — the event drops and doesn't come back

4G/5G modem configured and tested as automatic failover before any event.

Wrong pixel pitch — the audience sees pixelation from 5 meters away

Calculate: minimum distance (m) = pixel pitch (mm) × 3. Adjust the pitch to the venue.

IMAG latency above 3 frames — the artist sees a ghost of themselves on the screen

Measure the latency of the complete chain: camera → switcher → processor → screen. Max 2 frames.

Not recording locally — streaming drops and there is no record of the event

Record on the local encoder + on the switcher + on a dedicated camera. Three copies before the event ends.

Cutting to the wrong camera during the guitar solo — an audience shot at the dramatic moment

Study the setlist before the show. Know the right camera moments for each song.

Not having an organized content folder — the VJ searches for a clip during the show

A clip library organized by BPM and emotional state, indexed before the show begins.

🎯  Conclusion — Video is what the audience will remember seeing

Professional video at events is not decoration — it is the interface between what happens on stage and what the audience perceives. At a show for 30,000 people, most are not close enough to see the artist's face without the IMAG system. The video system is what delivers the complete experience to everyone.


The events video market is expanding rapidly. Live streaming, hybrid events, LED walls at every production size, creative VJing as a differentiator — the demand for qualified professionals exceeds the supply. Whoever masters this field with technical seriousness and a professional attitude will find a market that is waiting for them.


🏅  The path to becoming an events video professional

LEVEL 1 — LED Technician: assemble, calibrate and operate panels. Learn NovaStar processors. (0 to 6 months)

LEVEL 2 — Switcher Operator: master the Blackmagic ATEM. Operate small events. (6 months to 2 years)

LEVEL 3 — Cameraman: learn composition, musical timing and communication via intercom. (1 to 3 years)

LEVEL 4 — VJ: master Resolume, create content in real time, integrate with lighting. (1 to 3 years)

LEVEL 5 — Video Director: plan complete systems, direct the team, sign off on projects. (5+ years)


There is no shortcut — each level builds the next. The director who does not know how to operate a switcher has no technical authority over the team.


"The stage happens once.

The image you capture from that stage — lasts forever."


SMU Produções — Professional School of Events

e6f87a55-32e5-40d2-9ad0-b6ed17374752.conteudo_rico:
MODULE 6 — PRODUCTION

The producer is the conductor who leads the entire orchestra of an event. It is the producer who turns the idea into reality, coordinating people, resources and deadlines.

6.1 Types of Producer

Role

Scope

Profile

Executive Producer

Strategy, budget, hiring, macro decisions

Visionary, negotiator, financial manager

Field Producer

On-site execution, coordination of suppliers, problem solving

Organizer, practical, communicator

Technical Producer

Interface between production and the technical teams (sound, light, video)

Technical knowledge + management

Stage Manager

Stage coordination — everything that happens on stage goes through them

Timing, communication, cool head

Tour Manager

Coordinates tour logistics (transport, hotel, meals)

Logistics, languages, planning


6.2 The Producer's Tools

General schedule (event timeline); Stage plot; Technical and hospitality rider; Detailed budget by line item; Operational checklist; Contingency plan; Contact list (phone numbers of EVERYONE involved); and HT radio (instant communication). The producer lives on spreadsheets, lists and constant communication.

6.3 Legal Obligations

The producer must ensure: permits and licenses (City Hall, Fire Department, Military Police, ECAD); civil liability insurance; regular hiring of staff (CLT or RPA); payment of copyright royalties; fire brigade and ambulance; and compliance with safety standards (NRs).

444de2a7-b7ce-4ca1-9dab-6d7994561ae8.conteudo_rico:
LESSON 7 — SOUND (AUDIO ENGINEERING)

Sound is the essence of any musical event or event with amplified speech. The audio professional is responsible for making the audience hear with clarity and quality.

7.1 Roles in the Sound Area

Role

Description

Sound Engineer (FOH)

Operates the front-of-house mixing console (Front of House) — what the AUDIENCE hears

Monitor Engineer

Operates the monitor console — what the MUSICIANS hear on stage

System Tech

Configures, aligns and optimizes the P.A. system (speakers, amplifiers, DSP)

Stage Technician (Audio)

Handles the patch bay, positions microphones, solves cabling problems

RF Tech

Manages wireless microphones and in-ears (frequencies, batteries, interference)


7.2 Main Equipment

Mixing console (Yamaha CL/QL, DiGiCo, Allen & Heath dLive, Midas, Soundcraft); P.A. — speaker system for the audience (Line Array: d&b, L-Acoustics, JBL VTX, Meyer Sound); Stage monitors (wedges) and In-Ear systems (Shure PSM, Sennheiser); Microphones (Shure SM58/SM57/Beta, Sennheiser, AKG, DPA); DI Boxes (converter from unbalanced to balanced signal); DSP processors (equalization, compression, delay, limiter); and Power amplifiers (Crown, Lab Gruppen, Powersoft).

7.3 Fundamental Concepts

Gain vs. Volume: gain is the amplification at the channel input; volume is the output level. Adjust the gain first, then the fader. Equalization (EQ): adjustment of frequencies (lows, mids, highs) for clarity. Compression: dynamic control — reduces peaks and evens out the volume. Feedback (howling): when the P.A. sound returns to the microphone and creates a loop. Solved with EQ, positioning and technique. Line Array: system of vertically stacked speakers that projects sound over long distances with uniform coverage.
